instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, z is set to 9 and q3 is assigned z, so q3 starts at 9 and z equals 9 throughout. Since z is never modified inside the loop, its value remains 9 across all iterations. The loop body decreases q3 by 3, and the guard q3-3 >= 0 requires q3 to be at least 3 before each iteration. Starting from 9 and repeatedly subtracting 3 preserves divisibility by 3, so q3 % 3 == 0 holds after each iteration. Because we begin at 9 and only subtract, q3 never exceeds 9 and cannot become negative, ensuring 0 <= q3 <= 9 throughout the loop. Therefore, the properties z == 9, q3 % 3 == 0, and 0 <= q3 <= 9 hold as invariants, with q3 being the only variable updated in the loop. int main1(){ int z, q3; z=9; q3=z; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant z == 9; loop invariant q3 % 3 == 0; loop invariant 0 <= q3 <= 9; loop assigns q3; */ while (q3-3>=0) { q3 = q3 - 3; } }
rejected: I observe that at loop entry the guard q3-3>=0 implies q3>=3, so in particular q3>=0 holds when the loop begins. The guard is rechecked before each iteration and after the body q3 = q3 - 3 the fact that the previous value was at least 3 guarantees the new value is nonnegative, so q3>=0 is preserved. Every iteration performs an exact subtraction of 3, so after k iterations the value is initial_q3 - 3*k and thus the value at any point is obtained by repeatedly subtracting 3. From that representation I see that q3 stays in the same congruence class modulo 3 as the initial value, and in the reachable states inside the loop q3 therefore takes values that are multiples of 3, giving q3 % 3 == 0. If q3 is a multiple of 3 then it can be written as three times its integer quotient by 3, so q3 == 3*(q3/3) holds throughout the loop. /*@ loop invariant q3 >= 0; loop invariant q3 % 3 == 0; loop invariant q3 == 3*(q3/3); loop assigns q3; loop variant q3; */ while (q3-3>=0) { q3 = q3 - 3; }

Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initialization mgq = (v%7) + 15, qt = 0, and k = -6, I seed the state with known base values. With qt = 0, the relation k = -6 + 3*qt holds at the start. Each loop iteration increases k by 3, v by 1, and qt by 1, so after qt iterations we have k = -6 + 3*qt. Since mgq is fixed before the loop as (v_pre % 7) + 15, mgq remains equal to (\at(v,Pre) % 7) + 15 throughout. Also each iteration increases v by 1, so v = v_pre + qt, i.e., v == \at(v,Pre) + qt. Because the guard enforces qt <= mgq - 1 before an iteration, after the iteration qt' = qt + 1 satisfies qt' <= mgq, so qt <= mgq holds as an invariant. Collecting these, the loop invariants are k == -6 + 3*qt, v == \at(v,Pre) + qt, mgq == (\at(v,Pre) % 7) + 15, and qt <= mgq. int main1(int v){ int mgq, qt, k; mgq=(v%7)+15; qt=0; k=-6; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant k == -6 + 3*qt; loop invariant v == \at(v, Pre) + qt; loop invariant mgq == (\at(v, Pre) % 7) + 15; loop invariant qt <= mgq; loop assigns k, v, qt; */ while (1) { if (!(qt<=mgq-1)) { break; } k = k + 3; v++; qt++; } }
rejected: From the per-iteration updates, I see that if the loop starts with qt0 = 0, k0 = -6, and v0 = at(v,Pre), then after t iterations qt = t, k = -6 + 3t, and v = at(v,Pre) + t. Therefore I deduce k == -6 + 3*qt holds for all iterations. Because the loop continues only while qt <= mgq - 1, I know at the top of each iteration qt <= mgq, so qt <= mgq is preserved as an invariant. Since v increments by 1 each iteration while qt also increments by 1, I deduce that v - qt remains equal to its initial value, i.e., v == at(v,Pre) + qt. The loop variant mgq - qt decreases by 1 each iteration due to the qt++ update, ensuring progress toward termination. Thus initialization plus the updates and guard yield the invariants k == -6 + 3*qt, v == at(v,Pre) + qt, and qt <= mgq, with mgq - qt as the decreasing variant. /*@ loop invariant k == -6 + 3*qt; loop invariant v == \at(v, Pre) + qt; loop invariant qt <= mgq; loop assigns k, v, qt; loop variant mgq - qt; */ while (1) { if (!(qt<=mgq-1)) { break; } k = k + 3; v++; qt++; }

Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: I compute g = 1*2 so g == 2, q = g so q == 2 initially, and rmn = q so rmn == 2 at initialization. The loop guard q-1 >= 0 means q >= 1 at the loop head, and since q starts at 2 and is decremented by 1 each iteration it never exceeds 2 nor becomes negative, so 0 <= q <= 2 holds. Each iteration performs rmn = rmn + q followed by rmn += 2, so rmn increases by q + 2 each iteration and after t iterations rmn = 2 + sum_{j=0}^{t-1} ((2 - j) + 2). I simplify the sum as sum_{j=0}^{t-1} (4 - j) = 4t - t*(t-1)/2, giving rmn = 2 + 4t - t*(t-1)/2 after t iterations. Substituting t = 2 - q (the number of iterations already performed when q has the current value) yields rmn == 2 + 4*(2 - q) - ((2 - q)*(1 - q))/2, while g remains 2 because it is never modified. int main1(){ int g, q, rmn; g=1*2; q=g; rmn=q; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant g == 2; loop invariant 0 <= q <= 2; loop invariant rmn == 2 + 4*(2 - q) - ((2 - q) * (1 - q)) / 2; loop assigns rmn, q; */ for (; q-1>=0; q = q - 1) { rmn = rmn + q; rmn += 2; } }
rejected: After initialization, q equals 2 and rmn equals 2, so the relations rmn >= q and q >= 1 hold at loop entry. The loop guard q-1 >= 0 implies q >= 1, so during every iteration the property q >= 1 remains true. In each iteration, q becomes q - 1 and rmn becomes rmn + q + 2, i.e., rmn' = rmn + q + 2 and q' = q - 1. If rmn >= q before an iteration, then rmn' - q' = (rmn + q + 2) - (q - 1) = rmn + 3, which is nonnegative, so rmn' >= q' after the iteration. Since both q and rmn are updated inside the loop, the analysis assigns loop assigns rmn, q. Because q decreases by 1 each iteration and is bounded below by 0 due to the guard, the loop variant can be taken as q. int main1(){ int g, q, rmn; g=1*2; q=g; rmn=q; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant rmn >= q; loop invariant q >= 1; loop assigns rmn, q; loop variant q; */ for (; q-1>=0; q = q - 1) { rmn = rmn + q; rmn += 2; } }

Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initialization, gv equals 5, xz5 equals -3, and jzd equals 10, which makes gv == 5, -3 <= xz5 <= gv, jzd >= 0, jzd >= xz5, (jzd + xz5) >= 7, and (jzd + xz5 + 1) % 8 == 0 hold at loop start. The loop guard uses xz5 < gv with gv fixed at 5, so the loop executes while xz5 progresses from -3 up to 4, preserving the boundedness of xz5. In the body, xz5 becomes xz5 + 1 and jzd becomes 2*jzd + xz5, i.e., jzd' = 2*jzd_old + xz5_old and xz5' = xz5_old + 1. Since gv remains unchanged at 5, the invariant gv == 5 stays true after each iteration. Because xz5 only increases toward gv, the bound -3 <= xz5 <= gv persists throughout the loop. The sum S = jzd + xz5 evolves as S' = 2*S + 1, so from S = 7 we get S' >= 15, ensuring (jzd + xz5) >= 7 and (jzd + xz5 + 1) is still divisible by 8, while jzd >= xz5 holds because jzd' - xz5' = 2*jzd_old - 1 remains nonnegative after the first iteration. int main1(){ int gv, xz5, jzd; gv=1*5; xz5=-3; jzd=10; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant gv == 5; loop invariant -3 <= xz5 <= gv; loop invariant jzd >= 0; loop invariant jzd >= xz5; loop invariant (jzd + xz5) >= 7; loop invariant (jzd + xz5 + 1) % 8 == 0; loop assigns jzd, xz5; */ while (xz5
rejected: I observe that inside the loop the only modification to xz5 is xz5++, so its value increases by exactly 1 each iteration, and the loop condition xz5 < gv keeps the number of iterations finite. Therefore gv - xz5 decreases by 1 on every iteration, making it a natural loop variant. Since xz5 starts in the range [-3, 4] and is only incremented, its value remains within [-3, 4] throughout the loop, establishing -3 <= xz5 <= 4. The update jzd := jzd + jzd followed by jzd := jzd + xz5 yields jzd := 2*jzd + xz5; starting from a non-negative jzd and with xz5 bounded below, this recurrence preserves non-negativity, so jzd >= 0 holds after each iteration. Thus I derive the loop invariants jzd >= 0, -3 <= xz5 <= 4, and the loop variant gv - xz5. /*@ loop invariant jzd >= 0; loop invariant -3 <= xz5 <= 4; loop assigns jzd, xz5; loop variant gv - xz5; */ while (xz5<gv) { jzd += jzd; jzd = jzd + xz5; xz5++; }

chosen: I see initial assignments set bvv to 63, s to 0, zn to 0, and sy to -8, so I conclude bvv == 63, s == 0, sy == -8 and zn == 0 hold before the loop. From zn == 0 I conclude 0 <= zn initially, establishing the nonnegativity lower bound. The loop guard tests zn <= bvv - 1 before each iteration, so whenever the body executes I know zn <= bvv - 1 and after the body increments zn I infer zn <= bvv, thus the upper bound zn <= bvv is preserved. The loop body updates sy by sy = sy + s and increments zn by one while leaving s unchanged, so each iteration increases sy by s exactly when zn increases by one. Given the base case sy == -8 at zn == 0 and the per-iteration update sy := sy + s paired with zn := zn + 1, I deduce the general relation sy == -8 + zn * s for all loop states. Since s and bvv are never modified in the body, I maintain s == 0 and bvv == 63 throughout the loop. int main1(){ int bvv, s, zn, sy; bvv=63; s=0; zn=0; sy=-8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant s == 0; loop invariant bvv == 63; loop invariant sy == -8 + zn * s; loop invariant 0 <= zn; loop invariant zn <= bvv; loop assigns sy, zn; */ while (1) { if (!(zn<=bvv-1)) { break; } sy = sy + s; zn++; } }
rejected: At initialization I observe that s equals 0 and zn is nonnegative; because the loop body contains no assignment to s, I conclude s == 0 holds throughout the loop. The loop guard is zn <= bvv-1, so whenever the body executes I know zn <= bvv-1 and hence zn <= bvv. In the loop body sy = sy + s and zn++ are executed; the first leaves s unchanged and the second only increases zn, so zn never decreases from its initial nonnegative value. Combining these facts I obtain 0 <= zn && zn <= bvv during loop execution. Since zn increases by one each iteration, the quantity bvv - zn decreases by one on each iteration and remains nonnegative while the guard holds, so bvv - zn is a natural decreasing variant. /*@ loop invariant s == 0; loop invariant 0 <= zn && zn <= bvv; loop assigns sy, zn; loop variant bvv - zn; */ while (1) { if (!(zn<=bvv-1)) { break; } sy = sy + s; zn++; }

chosen: At initialization I set x = 1, sj = x and gv = sj so gv = 1, and I set uo = 12. From those initial values I infer uo = 3*gv + 9 (since 12 = 3*1 + 9) and uo >= 12. The loop header only assigns gv and uo, so x is never modified by the loop and I conclude x == 1 holds throughout. Each loop iteration performs uo = uo + 3 and gv += x; with x == 1 this makes uo increase by 3 and gv increase by 1, so the quantity uo - 3*gv is preserved at its initial value 9, hence uo == 3*gv + 9 remains true. Because uo starts at 12 and only increases by 3 each iteration, I conclude uo >= 12 holds in every loop state. int main1(){ int x, sj, uo, gv; x=1; sj=x; uo=12; gv=sj; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant x == 1; loop invariant uo == 3*gv + 9; loop invariant uo >= 12; loop assigns gv, uo; */ while (uo<=x-1) { uo = uo + 3; gv += x; } }
rejected: From the initialization we have x = 1, uo = 12, and gv = 1, so (uo - 12) equals 0 and is divisible by 3, and gv matches 1 + ((uo - 12)/3) * x when k = 0. Each loop iteration updates uo by +3 and gv by +x, so (uo - 12) increases by 3, preserving the property that (uo - 12) is a multiple of 3. If k iterations have occurred, uo = 12 + 3k and gv = 1 + k*x hold, establishing a concrete relationship between uo and gv. Consequently, gv = 1 + ((uo - 12)/3) * x follows from the previous line, since (uo - 12)/3 equals k. Therefore the two invariants ((uo - 12) % 3) == 0 and gv == 1 + ((uo - 12)/3) * x describe the maintained relationship through the loop updates. The loop assigns only uo and gv, which are the variables involved in these invariant relationships. int main1(){ int x, sj, uo, gv; x=1; sj=x; uo=12; gv=sj; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant ((uo - 12) % 3) == 0; loop invariant gv == 1 + ((uo - 12) / 3) * x; loop assigns uo, gv; */ while (uo<=x-1) { uo = uo + 3; gv += x; } }

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: At initialization I set x = 1, sj = x and gv = sj so gv = 1, and I set uo = 12. From those initial values I infer uo = 3*gv + 9 (since 12 = 3*1 + 9) and uo >= 12. The loop header only assigns gv and uo, so x is never modified by the loop and I conclude x == 1 holds throughout. Each loop iteration performs uo = uo + 3 and gv += x; with x == 1 this makes uo increase by 3 and gv increase by 1, so the quantity uo - 3*gv is preserved at its initial value 9, hence uo == 3*gv + 9 remains true. Because uo starts at 12 and only increases by 3 each iteration, I conclude uo >= 12 holds in every loop state. int main1(){ int x, sj, uo, gv; x=1; sj=x; uo=12; gv=sj; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant x == 1; loop invariant uo == 3*gv + 9; loop invariant uo >= 12; loop assigns gv, uo; */ while (uo<=x-1) { uo = uo + 3; gv += x; } }
rejected: From the initialization, I have x = 1, sj = x = 1, uo = 12, and gv = sj = 1; thus x equals 1 and uo equals 3*gv + 9 (since 3*1 + 9 = 12). The loop guard depends on uo and x, but with x fixed at 1 the guard reduces to uo <= 0, which is not satisfied by the initial uo, so the loop cannot violate these relations. In the loop body, I update uo to uo + 3 and gv to gv + x; since x = 1, this means uo increases by 3 and gv increases by 1. Therefore, after an iteration, uo' = uo + 3 and gv' = gv + 1, so 3*gv' + 9 = 3*(gv + 1) + 9 = uo + 3 = uo', preserving the equality uo' = 3*gv' + 9. Because I never change x, the relation x = 1 remains, and the preserved relation shows the loop invariant uo == 3*gv + 9 holds alongside x == 1. int main1(){ int x, sj, uo, gv; x=1; sj=x; uo=12; gv=sj; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant x == 1; loop invariant uo == 3*gv + 9; loop assigns gv, uo; */ while (uo<=x-1) { uo = uo + 3; gv += x; } }

Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, oj starts at 0, q starts at 5, and f and h are the input values that will be updated; xu is fixed at 64. The loop guard enforces that the loop runs only while oj < xu, so oj never exceeds xu during execution. In every iteration, we increment oj by 1, then add the new oj to q and to h, while f is increased by 2. After k iterations, with oj = k, q has become 5 plus the sum of 1 through k, and h has become the original h plus that same triangular sum, while f has become the original f plus 2k. Therefore q equals 5 plus oj times (oj plus 1) divided by 2, h equals the original h plus oj times (oj plus 1) divided by 2, f equals the original f plus 2 times oj, and 0 <= oj <= xu holds at all times. int main1(int f,int h){ int xu, dcdv, oj, q; xu=64; dcdv=0; oj=dcdv; q=5; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant q == 5 + (oj*(oj+1))/2; loop invariant f == \at(f,Pre) + 2*oj; loop invariant h == \at(h,Pre) + (oj*(oj+1))/2; loop invariant 0 <= oj <= xu; loop assigns oj, q, f, h; */ while (1) { if (!(oj
rejected: I observe that before the loop begins, oj has some initial value and xu is fixed, establishing a counter-like relationship that will be updated inside the loop. The loop guard ensures that at the start of every iteration we have oj < xu, and since the body only increments oj, oj will never exceed xu within the loop. Therefore, across iterations oj remains within a nonnegative bound up to xu (0 <= oj <= xu, assuming the initial oj is nonnegative). In the loop body, f is increased by 2 while oj is increased by 1, and q and h accumulate the current oj, so each iteration adds 2 to f and 1 to oj. As a result, the quantity f − 2*oj stays equal to its initial value (Pre_f − 2*Pre_oj), which, with the usual assumption that Pre_oj is zero, yields f = Pre_f + 2*oj. Finally, because oj is incremented by 1 each iteration and the loop stops when oj reaches xu, the remaining distance xu − oj decreases by 1 each time, giving the loop variant (xu − oj). /*@ loop invariant 0 <= oj && oj &<= xu; loop invariant f == \at(f,Pre) + 2*oj; loop assigns oj, q, f, h; loop variant (xu - oj); */ while (1) { if (!(oj<xu)) { break; } oj++; q = q + oj; f += 2; h = h + oj; }

Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, oj starts at 0, q starts at 5, and f and h are the input values that will be updated; xu is fixed at 64. The loop guard enforces that the loop runs only while oj < xu, so oj never exceeds xu during execution. In every iteration, we increment oj by 1, then add the new oj to q and to h, while f is increased by 2. After k iterations, with oj = k, q has become 5 plus the sum of 1 through k, and h has become the original h plus that same triangular sum, while f has become the original f plus 2k. Therefore q equals 5 plus oj times (oj plus 1) divided by 2, h equals the original h plus oj times (oj plus 1) divided by 2, f equals the original f plus 2 times oj, and 0 <= oj <= xu holds at all times. int main1(int f,int h){ int xu, dcdv, oj, q; xu=64; dcdv=0; oj=dcdv; q=5; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant q == 5 + (oj*(oj+1))/2; loop invariant f == \at(f,Pre) + 2*oj; loop invariant h == \at(h,Pre) + (oj*(oj+1))/2; loop invariant 0 <= oj <= xu; loop assigns oj, q, f, h; */ while (1) { if (!(oj
rejected: At loop start, the variables satisfy f = \at(f,Pre), h = \at(h,Pre), oj = 0, and xu = 64, while q is 5. The loop continues only while oj < xu, so the value of oj never exceeds xu across iterations. In each iteration, oj is increased by 1, then q is increased by the new oj, and f is increased by 2 while h is increased by the new oj. After k iterations, oj = k and f = f_pre + 2k, hence f = \at(f,Pre) + 2*oj. After k iterations, h = h_pre + k(k+1)/2, so h = \at(h,Pre) + (oj*(oj+1))/2. Consequently 0 <= oj <= xu holds and the loop assigns oj, q, f, h. int main1(int f,int h){ int xu, dcdv, oj, q; xu=64; dcdv=0; oj=dcdv; q=5; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant f == \at(f,Pre) + 2*oj; loop invariant h == \at(h,Pre) + (oj*(oj+1))/2; loop invariant 0 <= oj <= xu; loop assigns oj, q, f, h; */ while (1) { if (!(oj

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization I have nm = 3 and k = 0, so before any iterations nm is 3 and k is 0. The loop guard plus the inner updates increment nm by 1 each iteration, so nm takes values 4, 5, ..., up to sr; thus 3 <= nm <= sr holds throughout. In each iteration k is increased first by the new nm and then by 2*q, so after t iterations k equals the sum of the t nm-values encountered plus 2*q per iteration. If I let t be the number of iterations, then nm = 3 + t and k = sum_{i=1..t} (3+i) + 2q t, which simplifies to k = t(t+1)/2 + (3+2q) t. Substituting t = nm - 3 yields k = ((nm-3)(nm-2))/2 + (3+2q)(nm-3), and this can be rewritten equivalently as 2*k = (nm-3)*(nm + 4*q + 4). int main1(int q){ int sr, nm, k; sr=18; nm=3; k=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 2*k == (nm - 3) * (nm + 4*q + 4); loop invariant 3 <= nm <= sr; loop invariant k == ((nm - 3) * (nm - 2)) / 2 + (nm - 3) * (3 + 2 * q); loop invariant k == (nm*nm + (4*q + 1)*nm - 12*(q + 1)) / 2; loop invariant k == (nm*(nm+1))/2 + 2*q*nm - 6 - 6*q; loop invariant 3 <= nm && nm <= sr; loop invariant k == (nm - 3) * (2 * q + 4) + ((nm - 3) * (nm - 4)) / 2; loop invariant 3 <= nm; loop invariant nm <= sr; loop assigns k, nm; */ while (nm<=sr-1) { nm = nm + 1; k = k + nm; k = k+q+q; } }
rejected: I observe that the initialization sets nm to 3, k to 0, and sr to 18, so with t = nm - 3 = 0 the expression for k evaluates to 0, matching the initial k value. I deduce that 3 <= nm holds at loop entry and will be preserved because nm is only ever increased by 1 inside the loop. I note that the loop guard uses nm and sr to bound progression, so as nm increases the invariant’s dependence on nm remains well-defined within the loop, supporting its preservation. I see that in each iteration nm becomes nm + 1, so t = nm - 3 becomes t + 1. I verify that k is updated by k := k + nm after nm has been incremented, which amounts to increasing k by the new nm, plus an additional 2q, and that this delta matches the change required by the invariant when t increases by 1. Consequently, after each iteration, k equals (nm - 3) * (2q + 4) + ((nm - 3) * (nm - 4)) / 2, and nm remains at least 3 while the loop progresses, with sr - nm providing a decreasing loop variant. int main1(int q){ int sr, nm, k; sr=18; nm=3; k=0; /*@ loop invariant k == (nm - 3) * (2 * q + 4) + ((nm - 3) * (nm - 4)) / 2; loop invariant 3 <= nm; loop assigns k, nm; loop variant sr - nm; @*/ while (nm<=sr-1) { nm = nm + 1; k = k + nm; k = k+q+q; } }

rejected: I start with nm = 3, k = 0, and sr = 18, so the base case 2*k == (nm - 3) * (nm + 4*q + 4) yields 0 == 0 and 3 <= nm holds. I observe that the loop guard nm <= sr - 1 keeps nm at least 3 and, after each iteration, nm increases by 1, preserving 3 <= nm <= sr throughout execution. In the loop body, nm becomes nm + 1 and k is increased by nm, then by q + q, so the total k increment is nm + 2q with nm the newly updated value. Therefore, 3 <= nm <= sr and the k–nm relations remain true throughout the loop, derived from initialization and the per-iteration updates. int main1(int q){ int sr, nm, k; sr=18; nm=3; k=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 2*k == (nm - 3) * (nm + 4*q + 4); loop invariant 3 <= nm <= sr; loop invariant k == (nm*nm + (4*q + 1)*nm - 12*(q + 1)) / 2; loop invariant 3 <= nm && nm <= sr; loop invariant k == (nm - 3) * (2 * q + 4) + ((nm - 3) * (nm - 4)) / 2; loop invariant 3 <= nm; loop assigns k, nm; */ while (nm<=sr-1) { nm = nm + 1; k = k + nm; k = k+q+q; } }

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: I start from the initial assignments hc = 0, s = -17004, and c = 6, and I call the initial value of b as b0 and of p as p0. I observe that each loop iteration performs s = s + c - 2, b = b + 1, p = p + hc, and c = c + 1, so s increases by the current (c - 2), b increments by 1, p is increased by 0 because hc is 0, and c increments by 1. After k iterations s equals s0 + sum_{i=0..k-1}(6 + i - 2) = s0 + sum_{i=0..k-1}(4 + i). I compute the sum to get s = s0 + 4k + k(k-1)/2 = s0 + k(k+7)/2. Because b increments by 1 each iteration, k = b - b0, and since c starts at 6 and increments by 1 each iteration, c = 6 + k so (b - b0) = (c - 6) and c >= 6. Finally, hc never changes from 0 and p is only incremented by hc, so p remains equal to p0 and hc stays 0. int main1(int b,int p){ int hc, s, c; hc=0; s=-17004; c=6; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant s == -17004 + ((b - \at(b, Pre)) * (b - \at(b, Pre) + 7)) / 2; loop invariant p == \at(p, Pre); loop invariant (b - \at(b, Pre)) == (c - 6); loop invariant hc == 0; loop invariant c >= 6; loop assigns b, p, s, c; */ while (s<=-3) { s = s+c-2; b += 1; p = p + hc; c = c + 1; } }
rejected: I initialize hc to 0, s to -17004, and c to 6 while b has some initial value b_pre, so at loop entry b - b_pre = 0 and therefore c equals 6 + (b - b_pre). p is not modified during initialization and the only update inside the loop is p = p + hc with hc = 0, so p remains equal to its initial value p_pre. The loop guard is s <= -3 and since s starts at -17004 the guard holds initially and controls repetition while s stays <= -3. Each loop iteration increments b by 1 and increments c by 1, so the quantity c - b is preserved and thus c = 6 + (b - b_pre) holds after every iteration. Each iteration updates p by adding hc, but because hc is always zero p stays equal to p_pre throughout the loop. int main1(int b,int p){ int hc, s, c; hc=0; s=-17004; c=6; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant c == 6 + (b - \at(b, Pre)); loop invariant p == \at(p, Pre); loop assigns b, p, s, c; loop variant -3 - s; */ while (s<=-3) { s = s+c-2; b += 1; p = p + hc; c = c + 1; } }

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization I observe that dqf is 1, g4l is 0, sgm is -8, and mm is 4, so I infer that dqf==1, g4l==0, and mm==4 should hold without being altered by the loop body. The loop guard uses sgm < dqf with dqf fixed at 1, so the loop continues only while sgm < 1, constraining sgm to remain at most dqf during execution. Inside the body, if sgm < dqf/2 (which is 0 under integer division), sgm increases by 2; otherwise it increases by 1; in all cases sgm is non-decreasing across iterations. Since sgm starts at -8 and never exceeds 1, the bounds -8 <= sgm <= dqf are preserved throughout. The update to mm is mm = mm + g4l, and with g4l == 0, mm remains 4 every iteration. Therefore the invariants dqf==1, g4l==0, mm==4, and -8 <= sgm <= dqf hold across the loop. int main1(){ int dqf, g4l, sgm, mm; dqf=1; g4l=0; sgm=-8; mm=4; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant dqf == 1; loop invariant g4l == 0; loop invariant mm == 4; loop invariant -8 <= sgm <= dqf; loop assigns sgm, mm; */ while (1) { if (!(sgm
rejected: From initialization I have dqf = 1, g4l = 0, sgm = -8 and mm = 4, so initially sgm is at least -8, sgm is at most dqf, and mm - 4 = 0 which is divisible by any nonzero g4l. The loop guard while(sgm < dqf) means that at the moment the body executes I have dqf - sgm > 0 and sgm < dqf. In the body sgm is increased either by 2 when sgm < dqf/2 or by 1 otherwise, so sgm is nondecreasing from its initial value and therefore stays >= -8. The branch structure prevents overshooting dqf because when adding 2 is allowed the condition sgm < dqf/2 guarantees sgm+2 remains <= dqf, and when sgm is closer to dqf the else adds only 1, so sgm stays <= dqf. Each loop iteration also does mm = mm + g4l, so mm - 4 accumulates integer multiples of g4l and hence (when g4l != 0) remains divisible by g4l. Since sgm increases by at least 1 each iteration, dqf - sgm starts positive and strictly decreases on each iteration, making dqf - sgm a valid loop variant. int main1(){ int dqf, g4l, sgm, mm; dqf=1; g4l=0; sgm=-8; mm=4; /*@ loop invariant sgm >= -8; loop invariant sgm <= dqf; loop invariant (g4l != 0) ==> ((mm - 4) % g4l == 0); loop assigns sgm, mm; loop variant dqf - sgm; @*/ while (1) { if (!(sgm

chosen: I examine initialization: to4 is set to the initial value of i plus 14, r66n and a9 are zero, and c starts at its initial value, so the base case for each invariant holds. I observe the loop guard r66n < to4 and since to4 is not modified it provides a fixed bound derived from the initial i. I note that each iteration increments r66n by 2 and c by 1, so starting from r66n=0 and c=c_initial this yields by induction r66n = 2*(c - c_initial). I track i: on each iteration i is increased by the new r66n, so after k iterations i = initial_i + sum_{j=1..k} 2j = initial_i + k*(k+1), and with k = c - c_initial this gives i = initial_i + (c - c_initial)*((c - c_initial)+1). I observe c increases by one each iteration, hence c >= c_initial. I observe a9 increases by 5 when r66n increases by 2, so 2*a9 and 5*r66n both increase by 10 each iteration and remain equal, yielding 2*a9 = 5*r66n. int main1(int i,int c){ int to4, r66n, a9; to4=i+14; r66n=0; a9=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant to4 == \at(i, Pre) + 14; loop invariant r66n == 2*(c - \at(c, Pre)); loop invariant i == \at(i, Pre) + (c - \at(c, Pre)) * ((c - \at(c, Pre)) + 1); loop invariant c >= \at(c, Pre); loop invariant 2*a9 == 5*r66n; loop assigns r66n, a9, i, c; */ while (r66n

The loop guard r66n < to4 bounds the iterations, so r66n remains below a fixed nonnegative limit as the loop executes. In the loop body, r66n increases by 2 and a9 increases by 5, while i accumulates r66n and c increases by 1. Since r66n is increased by 2 each iteration, floor(r66n/2) increases by exactly 1 per iteration, so preserving a9 = 5 * (r66n/2) after the updates. Similarly, c increases by 1 while r66n/2 increases by 1, hence c remains equal to Pre(c) + (r66n/2) after each iteration. The updates also preserve r66n >= 0 and create a decreasing loop variant to4 - r66n because r66n grows by 2 every time. Finally, the loop body modifies exactly r66n, a9, i, and c, which matches the variables appearing in the invariants. /*@ loop invariant a9 == 5 * (r66n/2); loop invariant c == \at(c,Pre) + (r66n/2); loop invariant r66n >= 0; loop variant to4 - r66n; loop assigns r66n, a9, i, c; */ while (r66n<to4) { r66n += 2; a9 = a9 + 5; i += r66n; c = c + 1; }

The y update adds ngr + 3, so after i iterations y equals -16845 plus the sum of (ngr_t + 3) for t = 0 to i-1, i.e., y + 16845 equals sum of (-2 + 3t). Consequently 6*(y + 16845) equals the sum of 9t^2 - 21t, which matches (ngr + 5)*(ngr - 2) when ngr = -5 + 3i, since (ngr + 5)*(ngr - 2) evaluates to (3i)*(3i - 7) = 9i^2 - 21i. Therefore the invariant 6*(y + 16845) == (ngr + 5)*(ngr - 2) holds at loop heads and is preserved by the body updates. int main1(){ int y, ngr; y=-16845; ngr=-5; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant (ngr + 5) % 3 == 0; loop invariant ngr >= -5; loop invariant 6*(y + 16845) == (ngr + 5)*(ngr - 2); loop assigns y, ngr; */ while (1) { if (!(y<=-3)) { break; } y = y+ngr+3; ngr += 2; ngr = ngr + 1; } }
rejected: I start with y = -16845 and ngr = -5, so ngr >= -5 holds and 6*(y + 16845) = 6*0 = 0 = (ngr + 5)*(ngr - 2). The loop guard y <= -3 only controls whether the body runs and does not change variables, so it does not affect these relations prior to an iteration. In the body I compute y' = y + ngr + 3 and ngr' = ngr + 3, hence 6*(y' + 16845) = 6*(y + 16845) + 6*(ngr + 3), and substituting 6*(y + 16845) = (ngr + 5)*(ngr - 2) gives 6*(y' + 16845) = (ngr + 5)*(ngr - 2) + 6*(ngr + 3) = (ngr + 8)*(ngr + 1) = (ngr' + 5)*(ngr' - 2). From ngr >= -5 I deduce ngr' = ngr + 3 >= -2, so ngr' >= -5, and thus both the quadratic relation and the lower bound are preserved by the update. int main1(){ int y, ngr; y=-16845; ngr=-5; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant ngr >= -5; loop invariant 6*(y + 16845) == (ngr + 5)*(ngr - 2); loop assigns y, ngr; */ while (1) { if (!(y<=-3)) { break; } y = y+ngr+3; ngr += 2; ngr = ngr + 1; } }

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initialization, ge is set to f - 9, m5n is 0, and wi is set to ko which equals ge, so at loop entry ge equals the original f value minus 9, and m5n starts at 0 with wi equal to ge. Since ge = f - 9, it follows that ge + 9 equals the original value of f, i.e., \at(f, Pre); this yields the invariant ge + 9 == \at(f, Pre). The loop body updates f by adding 2 each iteration, so f never decreases and its difference from the original Pre value remains even; hence (f - \at(f, Pre)) is even and f >= \at(f, Pre). The loop guard breaks when m5n is not less than ge, so the loop continues only while m5n < ge; therefore at loop head we have 0 <= m5n and, depending on ge, either m5n <= ge holds or, if ge <= 0, the condition ge <= 0 makes the invariant hold. Since m5n increases by 1 or 2, it remains nonnegative throughout, and since ge is fixed, the relation (m5n <= ge || ge <= 0) persists as long as the loop condition is considered. Finally, wi starts at ge and is incremented by m5n, which is nonnegative, so wi >= ge, and since ge = Pre_f - 9, we obtain wi >= \at(f, Pre) - 9. int main1(int f){ int ge, ko, m5n, wi; ge=f-9; ko=ge; m5n=0; wi=ko; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant (f - \at(f, Pre)) % 2 == 0; loop invariant f >= \at(f, Pre); loop invariant wi >= \at(f, Pre) - 9; loop invariant 0 <= m5n && (m5n <= ge || ge <= 0); loop invariant ge + 9 == \at(f, Pre); loop assigns m5n, f, wi; */ while (1) { if (!(m5n
rejected: From initialization I observe ge is assigned f-9, and m5n is set to 0 while f equals its initial value, so ge becomes the initial f minus 9 and f initially equals \at(f,Pre). Therefore ge == \at(f,Pre) - 9 and f == \at(f,Pre) at entry. Consequently the initial difference f - \at(f,Pre) is 0, which implies ((f - \at(f,Pre)) % 2 == 0) holds initially. Since ge is never modified in the loop, the relation ge == \at(f,Pre) - 9 persists throughout the iterations, and f only increases, so f >= \at(f,Pre) is preserved. The loop body increases f by 2 each iteration, so after k iterations f = \at(f,Pre) + 2k, which keeps (f - \at(f,Pre)) even. The loop continues while m5n < ge, and m5n is increased by 1 or 2 each step, making ge - m5n decrease and remain nonnegative, giving ge - m5n as a decreasing variant. Finally, the loop body updates m5n, f, and wi, so the statement that the loop assigns m5n, f, wi is satisfied. int main1(int f){ int ge, ko, m5n, wi; ge=f-9; ko=ge; m5n=0; wi=ko; /*@ = m5n; loop invariant ge == \at(f, Pre) - 9 && f >= \at(f, Pre) && ((f - \at(f, Pre)) % 2 == 0); loop assigns m5n, f, wi; loop variant ge - m5n; */ while (1) { if (!(m5n<ge)) { break; } if (m5n<ge/2) { m5n += 2; } else { m5n += 1; } f += 2; wi += m5n; } }

Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initialization, ge is set to f - 9, m5n is 0, and wi is set to ko which equals ge, so at loop entry ge equals the original f value minus 9, and m5n starts at 0 with wi equal to ge. Since ge = f - 9, it follows that ge + 9 equals the original value of f, i.e., \at(f, Pre); this yields the invariant ge + 9 == \at(f, Pre). The loop body updates f by adding 2 each iteration, so f never decreases and its difference from the original Pre value remains even; hence (f - \at(f, Pre)) is even and f >= \at(f, Pre). The loop guard breaks when m5n is not less than ge, so the loop continues only while m5n < ge; therefore at loop head we have 0 <= m5n and, depending on ge, either m5n <= ge holds or, if ge <= 0, the condition ge <= 0 makes the invariant hold. Since m5n increases by 1 or 2, it remains nonnegative throughout, and since ge is fixed, the relation (m5n <= ge || ge <= 0) persists as long as the loop condition is considered. Finally, wi starts at ge and is incremented by m5n, which is nonnegative, so wi >= ge, and since ge = Pre_f - 9, we obtain wi >= \at(f, Pre) - 9. int main1(int f){ int ge, ko, m5n, wi; ge=f-9; ko=ge; m5n=0; wi=ko; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant (f - \at(f, Pre)) % 2 == 0; loop invariant f >= \at(f, Pre); loop invariant wi >= \at(f, Pre) - 9; loop invariant 0 <= m5n && (m5n <= ge || ge <= 0); loop invariant ge + 9 == \at(f, Pre); loop assigns m5n, f, wi; */ while (1) { if (!(m5n
rejected: From the initialization ge = f - 9, ko = ge, m5n = 0, and wi = ko, I infer that the pre-state value of f satisfies f_pre = ge + 9 and that m5n starts at 0 while ge remains constant. Therefore ge + 9 equals the value of f at loop entry, i.e., \at(f, Pre) = ge + 9. Since ge is never updated inside the loop, f can only increase by 2 each iteration (f += 2), so f never decreases relative to its pre-state: f >= \at(f, Pre). The loop guard enforces m5n < ge to continue, so the loop can only run while m5n stays strictly below ge; if ge <= 0 the loop does not execute, which justifies the conditional bound (m5n <= ge || ge <= 0) in the invariant. The body increases m5n by either 2 or 1, ensuring m5n remains nonnegative, which supports 0 <= m5n. Finally, wi is updated by wi += m5n, and the only variables assigned within the loop are m5n, f, and wi, matching the loop assigns clause. int main1(int f){ int ge, ko, m5n, wi; ge=f-9; ko=ge; m5n=0; wi=ko; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant f >= \at(f, Pre); loop invariant 0 <= m5n && (m5n <= ge || ge <= 0); loop invariant ge + 9 == \at(f, Pre); loop assigns m5n, f, wi; */ while (1) { if (!(m5n

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: I observe that qp is initialized to 0 and yc is initialized to 8, which gives a base case for the relation yc = 8 + qp. In each loop iteration, yc is incremented by 1 and qp is incremented by 1, so if yc = 8 + qp held before an iteration, it continues to hold after the iteration because yc' = yc + 1 and qp' = qp + 1 imply yc' = 8 + qp'. From the start, qp is 0 and is only ever increased, so 0 <= qp holds at every loop head. The loop continues only while qp + 1 <= ty, which constrains qp to never surpass ty, hence ty - qp remains nonnegative throughout the loop. Therefore, the invariants yc == 8 + qp, 0 <= qp, and ty - qp >= 0 are sustained by the initialization and the per-iteration updates. int main1(int t,int n){ int ty, qp, yc; ty=67; qp=0; yc=8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant yc == 8 + qp; loop invariant 0 <= qp; loop invariant ty - qp >= 0; loop assigns qp, yc; */ while (1) { if (!(qp+1<=ty)) { break; } yc++; qp = qp + 1; } }
rejected: I observe that initially ty is 67 and qp is 0, so 0 <= qp <= ty holds from the start. The loop only executes its body when qp+1 <= ty, which means inside the body qp <= ty-1, and after the update qp = qp + 1, so qp becomes at most ty and the relation qp <= ty is preserved. The loop body updates qp and yc, so the variables assigned by the loop are qp and yc. Since qp increases by 1 each iteration while ty stays fixed, the quantity ty - qp decreases by 1 every time, and it remains nonnegative. Therefore, throughout the loop we maintain 0 <= qp <= ty, ty - qp >= 0, and ty - qp serves as a decreasing loop variant while qp and yc are the loop-assigned variables. int main1(int t,int n){ int ty, qp, yc; ty=67; qp=0; yc=8; /*@ loop invariant 0 <= qp && qp <= ty; loop invariant ty - qp >= 0; loop assigns qp, yc; loop variant ty - qp; */ while (1) { if (!(qp+1<=ty)) { break; } yc++; qp = qp + 1; } }

chosen: At initialization, lr9 is 4, pf1c is 0, and vz is set to pf1c, so vz equals pf1c and the relation vz = 4 * pf1c holds for the starting state. Each loop iteration increases vz by 4 and pf1c by 1, so after an iteration vz grows by 4 while pf1c grows by 1. Consequently, if vz = 4 * pf1c before the iteration, then vz' = vz + 4 and pf1c' = pf1c + 1 satisfy vz' = 4 * pf1c', preserving the relation vz = 4 * pf1c across iterations. The loop exits when pf1c >= lr9, which with lr9 = 4 means pf1c cannot exceed 4 during execution, so pf1c <= lr9 holds throughout. Since pf1c starts at 0 and only increases, pf1c >= 0 also holds at all times. The loop only updates vz and pf1c, so the invariants tie vz directly to pf1c and bound pf1c by lr9. int main1(){ int lr9, pf1c, vz; lr9=1*4; pf1c=0; vz=pf1c; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant vz == 4 * pf1c; loop invariant pf1c <= lr9; loop invariant pf1c >= 0; loop assigns vz, pf1c; */ while (1) { vz += 4; pf1c += 1; if (pf1c>=lr9) { break; } } }
rejected: I observe that lr9 is set to 4, pf1c to 0, and vz is initialized to pf1c, so initially vz and pf1c are equal (both 0). Each loop iteration adds 4 to vz and 1 to pf1c, so if vz = 4*pf1c before an iteration, after the updates vz' = vz + 4 = 4*pf1c + 4 = 4*(pf1c + 1) = 4*pf1c', hence vz = 4*pf1c continues to hold after the iteration. Therefore the invariant vz == 4 * pf1c is preserved across iterations. Since pf1c starts at 0 and only increases by 1 each iteration, and lr9 is fixed at 4, pf1c <= lr9 remains true while the loop body executes. The difference lr9 - pf1c decreases by 1 each iteration, remaining non-negative until termination, giving a decreasing loop variant. int main1(){ int lr9, pf1c, vz; lr9=1*4; pf1c=0; vz=pf1c; /*@ loop invariant vz == 4 * pf1c; loop invariant pf1c <= lr9; loop assigns vz, pf1c; loop variant lr9 - pf1c; */ while (1) { vz += 4; pf1c += 1; if (pf1c>=lr9) { break; } } }

Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initial assignments ykpi=18, pj=3, n=ykpi, up=ykpi I observe that up equals ykpi, pj equals 3, and n equals ykpi. Because pj starts at 3 and the loop body only increments pj by 1, pj never decreases so I infer pj >= 3 throughout execution. The loop guard requires pj < ykpi to continue and pj is incremented by at most 1 per iteration, so pj cannot exceed ykpi and thus pj <= ykpi holds during the loop. Since up is not modified in the loop, I conclude up remains equal to ykpi for all iterations. The body updates n to n+up and pj to pj+1, so the quantity n - up*(pj-3) is preserved; it equals ykpi initially, hence n = ykpi + up*(pj - 3) holds throughout. int main1(){ int ykpi, pj, n, up; ykpi=18; pj=3; n=ykpi; up=ykpi; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant (up == ykpi); loop invariant n == ykpi + up*(pj - 3); loop invariant 3 <= pj <= ykpi; loop assigns n, pj; */ while (1) { if (!(pj
rejected: From the initialization, I see pj is set to 3, ykpi to 18, and n to ykpi while up is set to ykpi, so pj starts at 3 and up equals ykpi. The loop guard requires pj < ykpi, and the body increments pj by 1, so pj can only rise from 3 toward ykpi and never exceed it inside the loop. Therefore pj >= 3 and pj <= ykpi hold at each loop head. Since up is assigned once to ykpi and never modified in the loop, I deduce up == ykpi remains true throughout. The loop body updates only n and pj, so the invariant about the loop updating n and pj is justified. The quantity ykpi - pj decreases by 1 each iteration and stays non-negative due to the guard, giving a valid loop variant. int main1(){ int ykpi, pj, n, up; ykpi=18; pj=3; n=ykpi; up=ykpi; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant (pj >= 3); loop invariant (pj <= ykpi); loop invariant (up == ykpi); loop assigns n, pj; loop variant (ykpi - pj); */ while (1) { if (!(pj

Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initialization g = 1*2, we obtain g = 2 and zjd = 0. With zjd = 0, we see that zjd is a nonnegative multiple of 7 (0 % 7 = 0) and 0 <= zjd, and also 0 <= g. The loop guard zjd + 7 <= g restricts iterations to those where adding 7 does not exceed g, which implies zjd cannot surpass g during the loop. The loop update zjd = zjd + 7 preserves divisibility by 7, since adding 7 to a multiple of 7 yields another multiple of 7. The update also preserves nonnegativity, as zjd increases from a nonnegative value by a nonnegative amount. Therefore, the properties zjd % 7 == 0, zjd <= g, and 0 <= zjd are established by initialization and preserved by the loop body updates. int main1(){ int g, zjd; g=1*2; zjd=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant zjd % 7 == 0; loop invariant zjd <= g; loop invariant 0 <= zjd; loop assigns zjd; */ for (; zjd+7<=g; zjd = zjd + 7) { } }
rejected: From the initialization g = 1*2 and zjd = 0, I get g = 2 and zjd = 0, so zjd <= g holds initially. The loop guard zjd + 7 <= g implies, at loop entry, zjd <= g - 7, which provides a bound that governs the updates. Inside the body, zjd is updated to zjd + 7, so after the update zjd' = zjd + 7 <= g, preserving the relation zjd <= g across iterations. Since the loop body contains an assignment to zjd, the loop indeed assigns zjd. The loop variant g - zjd strictly decreases by 7 each iteration (because zjd increases by 7), and the guard ensures g - zjd >= 7 before the update, guaranteeing termination. Therefore I derive the loop invariants: zjd <= g; the loop assigns zjd; and the loop variant is g - zjd. int main1(){ int g, zjd; g=1*2; zjd=0; /*@ loop invariant zjd <= g; loop assigns zjd; loop variant g - zjd; @*/ for (; zjd+7<=g; zjd = zjd + 7) { } }

chosen: I observe the initialization sets fc to (y % 6) + 16, bdz0 to 0, and dv to y. From these initial values, the relation dv = y + 3 * bdz0 holds for the zero-iteration state. Also, at loop entry I have 0 <= bdz0 and, since fc is set once from y and never changed, fc = (y % 6) + 16 remains valid. The loop guard bdz0 + 1 <= fc ensures that before each iteration bdz0 <= fc - 1, and inside the loop body bdz0 is incremented by 1, so 0 <= bdz0 <= fc holds over all iterations. The body updates dv by +3 while bdz0 advances by +1, so after each iteration dv = y + 3 * bdz0 remains preserved. Finally, fc continues to equal (y % 6) + 16 throughout since it is not modified. int main1(int y){ int fc, bdz0, dv; fc=(y%6)+16; bdz0=0; dv=y; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant dv == y + 3 * bdz0; loop invariant 0 <= bdz0; loop invariant bdz0 <= fc; loop invariant fc == (y % 6) + 16; loop assigns dv, bdz0; */ for (; bdz0+1<=fc; bdz0 += 1) { dv = dv + 3; } }

Since fc is defined by the same expression that appears in the invariant fc == (param % 6) + 16, I can frame fc as equal to (param % 6) + 16 when param corresponds to y. The loop guard bdz0 + 1 <= fc enforces bdz0 < fc at each entry to the loop body, which together with the initial bdz0 = 0 yields 0 <= bdz0 and bdz0 <= fc as maintained facts. Each iteration increments bdz0 by 1, so bdz0 progresses from 0 toward fc in unit steps, preserving the bound against fc. The body updates dv by +3, and since dv is assigned in the loop, dv and bdz0 are the variables affected by the loop, matching the assigns clause. Consequently, fc - bdz0 decreases by 1 every iteration, establishing it as a valid loop variant. int main1(int y){ int fc, bdz0, dv; fc=(y%6)+16; bdz0=0; dv=y; /*@ loop invariant 0 <= bdz0 && bdz0 <= fc; loop invariant fc == (param % 6) + 16; loop assigns dv, bdz0; loop variant fc - bdz0; @*/ for (; bdz0+1<=fc; bdz0 += 1) { dv = dv + 3; } }
