Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization I see cr2 is x-5 and bh is x, while maf and ke start at 0 and so starts at 0, so the base facts cr2 == Pre(x) - 5, maf >= 0, so == 0, ke == maf*(maf-1)/2, and bh == Pre(x) + 3*maf and s == Pre(s) + maf*so hold at loop entry. The loop guard maf < cr2 implies the arithmetic fact cr2 - maf > 0, which will be true whenever the loop body executes. In the loop body ke is increased by the current maf, s is increased by so, maf is incremented by 1, and bh is increased by 3, preserving the linear relationship bh == Pre(x) + 3*maf and leaving cr2 constant. After k iterations, maf equals k, bh equals x + 3*k, ke equals k*(k-1)/2, and s equals Pre(s) + k*so, showing the closed forms that match the claimed invariants. Therefore the loop invariants persist: cr2 stays Pre(x) - 5, maf >= 0, so == 0, ke == maf*(maf-1)/2, bh == Pre(x) + 3*maf, and s == Pre(s) + maf*so, with the guard (maf < cr2) implying (cr2 - maf > 0). int main1(int s,int x){ int cr2, so, maf, ke, bh; cr2=x-5; so=0; maf=0; ke=0; bh=x; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant cr2 == \at(x, Pre) - 5; loop invariant maf >= 0; loop invariant so == 0; loop invariant ke == maf*(maf-1)/2; loop invariant bh == \at(x, Pre) + 3*maf; loop invariant s == \at(s, Pre) + maf*so; loop invariant (maf < cr2) ==> (cr2 - maf > 0); loop invariant bh == x + 3*maf; loop invariant cr2 == x - 5; loop assigns ke, s, maf, bh; */ while (1) { if (!(maf
rejected: I observe that at initialization cr2 is set to x minus five, so is set to zero, maf is set to zero, ke is set to zero, and bh is set to x, so I conclude cr2 equals the initial x minus five, so equals zero, and maf is nonnegative initially. The loop guard uses maf < cr2 and does not modify cr2 or so, so cr2 remains equal to the initial x minus five and so remains zero throughout the loop. Each loop iteration increments maf by one starting from zero, so maf always counts the number of completed iterations and is therefore >= 0. ke starts at zero and each iteration adds the previous maf, so ke equals the sum 0 + 1 + ... + (maf-1) which simplifies to maf*(maf-1)/2. s is increased by so each iteration and since so is constant zero, s equals its initial value plus maf*so. bh starts at x and is increased by three on each iteration, and because x is not modified, bh equals x + 3*maf while cr2 remains x - 5. int main1(int s,int x){ int cr2, so, maf, ke, bh; cr2=x-5; so=0; maf=0; ke=0; bh=x; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant cr2 == \at(x, Pre) - 5; loop invariant maf >= 0; loop invariant so == 0; loop invariant ke == maf*(maf-1)/2; loop invariant s == \at(s, Pre) + maf*so; loop invariant bh == x + 3*maf; loop invariant cr2 == x - 5; loop assigns ke, s, maf, bh; */ while (1) { if (!(maf

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: After initialization, fsy equals the initial w plus 12, y07 starts at 1, rrw and o02h start at 0, and the loop is allowed to run as long as y07 is not greater than fsy. Each iteration updates rrw by adding 2*y07 - 1 before y07 is incremented, so if rrw = (y07 - 1)^2 holds before the body, then after the update and the increment it becomes rrw' = (y07' - 1)^2, preserving rrw = (y07 - 1)^2. Meanwhile o02h starts at 0 and in the body it is increased by the new y07 value (after increment), giving o02h = sum of 2 through y07, which yields o02h = y07*(y07+1)/2 - 1. The w variable is increased by 3 each iteration after y07 is incremented, so w = Pre(w) + 3*(y07 - 1) holds at loop-entry. Since fsy is never updated, it remains equal to Pre(w) + 12 throughout. Finally, y07 remains at least 1 and only grows, so y07 >= 1 persists along with the guard relation y07 <= fsy. int main1(int w){ int fsy, jl, y07, rrw, o02h; fsy=w+12; jl=0; y07=1; rrw=0; o02h=jl; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant rrw == (y07 - 1) * (y07 - 1); loop invariant o02h == y07 * (y07 + 1) / 2 - 1; loop invariant w == \at(w, Pre) + 3 * (y07 - 1); loop invariant fsy == \at(w, Pre) + 12; loop invariant y07 >= 1; loop assigns rrw, y07, w, o02h; */ while (1) { if (!(y07<=fsy)) { break; } rrw = rrw+2*y07-1; y07 += 1; w = w + 3; o02h += y07; } }
rejected: I note that initially y07 is 1 and o02h is 0, and since the loop body adds the updated y07 to o02h, o02h follows the sequence that makes o02h == y07*(y07+1)/2 - 1 by induction. I observe that w increases by 3 every iteration and fsy is defined as at(w, Pre) + 12, so fsy remains a fixed offset from the initial w value and equals Pre(w) + 12. From the guard y07 <= fsy at the top of each iteration, I infer fsy - y07 >= 0, and since y07 starts at 1, this yields 1 <= y07 <= fsy + 1. During the body, y07 is incremented by 1, so the bound on y07 is preserved and the invariant 1 <= y07 <= fsy + 1 continues to hold at the next loop head. Because fsy is constant and y07 increases by 1 each iteration, the difference fsy - y07 decreases by 1, giving a valid loop variant that steadily diminishes. /*@ loop invariant o02h == y07 * (y07 + 1) / 2 - 1; loop invariant fsy == \at(w, Pre) + 12; loop invariant 1 <= y07 <= fsy + 1; loop invariant (fsy - y07) >= 0; loop assigns rrw, y07, w, o02h; loop variant fsy - y07; */ while (1) { if (!(y07<=fsy)) { break; } rrw = rrw+2*y07-1; y07 += 1; w = w + 3; o02h += y07; }

chosen: After initialization, fsy equals the initial w plus 12, y07 starts at 1, rrw and o02h start at 0, and the loop is allowed to run as long as y07 is not greater than fsy. Each iteration updates rrw by adding 2*y07 - 1 before y07 is incremented, so if rrw = (y07 - 1)^2 holds before the body, then after the update and the increment it becomes rrw' = (y07' - 1)^2, preserving rrw = (y07 - 1)^2. Meanwhile o02h starts at 0 and in the body it is increased by the new y07 value (after increment), giving o02h = sum of 2 through y07, which yields o02h = y07*(y07+1)/2 - 1. The w variable is increased by 3 each iteration after y07 is incremented, so w = Pre(w) + 3*(y07 - 1) holds at loop-entry. Since fsy is never updated, it remains equal to Pre(w) + 12 throughout. Finally, y07 remains at least 1 and only grows, so y07 >= 1 persists along with the guard relation y07 <= fsy. int main1(int w){ int fsy, jl, y07, rrw, o02h; fsy=w+12; jl=0; y07=1; rrw=0; o02h=jl; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant rrw == (y07 - 1) * (y07 - 1); loop invariant o02h == y07 * (y07 + 1) / 2 - 1; loop invariant w == \at(w, Pre) + 3 * (y07 - 1); loop invariant fsy == \at(w, Pre) + 12; loop invariant y07 >= 1; loop assigns rrw, y07, w, o02h; */ while (1) { if (!(y07<=fsy)) { break; } rrw = rrw+2*y07-1; y07 += 1; w = w + 3; o02h += y07; } }
rejected: From the initialization, fsy is set to the original w plus 12, y07 is 1, and rrw is 0 (with o02h initialized as well). Therefore rrw equals (y07 - 1)^2 initially, since (1 - 1)^2 is 0. Also, fsy equals the pre-state value of w plus 12, so the relation fsy == Pre(w) + 12 holds from the start. And y07 is initialized to 1, so y07 >= 1 holds at entry. In the loop body, rrw is updated by rrw + 2*y07 - 1 and y07 is incremented by 1; if rrw_old = (y07 - 1)^2, then rrw_new = (y07 - 1)^2 + 2*y07 - 1 = y07^2 and new_y07 = y07 + 1, which gives rrw_new = (new_y07 - 1)^2, hence rrw == (y07 - 1)^2 is preserved. Since fsy stays constant at Pre(w) + 12 and y07 increases, the loop variant fsy - y07 decreases by 1 each iteration, ensuring progress until the guard y07 <= fsy fails. int main1(int w){ int fsy, jl, y07, rrw, o02h; fsy=w+12; jl=0; y07=1; rrw=0; o02h=jl; /*@ loop invariant rrw == (y07 - 1) * (y07 - 1); loop invariant fsy == \at(w,Pre) + 12; loop invariant y07 >= 1; loop assigns w, rrw, o02h, y07; loop variant fsy - y07; @*/ while (1) { if (!(y07<=fsy)) { break; } rrw = rrw+2*y07-1; y07 += 1; w = w + 3; o02h += y07; } }

In the body, a8 increases by r, giving a8' = a8 + r, which with the prior a8 = r * of yields a8' = r * of'. In addition, tt increases by of^2, and since S(n) = ((n-1)n(2n-1))/6 satisfies S(n+1) - S(n) = n^2, we obtain tt' = 3 + S(of+1) = 3 + ((of' - 1) * of' * (2 * of' - 1)) / 6. Since r is unchanged, r == zp remains true, and the loop assigns a8, tt, of, c as it updates, preserving the stated invariant relationships across iterations. int main1(int c){ int zp, r, of, tt, a8; zp=28; r=zp; of=1; tt=3; a8=r; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant a8 == r * of; loop invariant tt == 3 + ((of - 1) * of * (2 * of - 1)) / 6; loop invariant r == zp; loop invariant 1 <= of <= zp + 1; loop assigns a8, tt, of, c; */ while (1) { if (!(of<=zp)) { break; } tt = tt+of*of; a8 += r; of = of + 1; c = c + c; } }

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initialization q = 1-10, c = 0, j = 0, qx = q, I compute q = -9, c = 0, j = 0, qx = -9. Therefore c == j % 4 holds since 0 == 0 % 4, and j >= 0 holds because j is 0. Also qx == q + j*(q + 2) holds since -9 = -9 + 0*( -9 + 2 ). The loop guard is j < q; with j = 0 and q = -9, the guard is false, so the body may not execute, but we continue to analyze the body for invariance. If the body were to execute once, c becomes (c+1) % 4, j becomes j+1, and qx becomes qx + q + 2, so qx' = qx + q + 2. Given c == j % 4 before, c' = (c+1) % 4 equals (j+1) % 4, preserving c == j % 4, and qx' = qx + q + 2 = q + (j+1)*(q+2), preserving qx == q + j*(q+2) with the updated j. int main1(){ int q, c, j, qx; q=1-10; c=0; j=0; qx=q; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant c == j % 4; loop invariant j >= 0; loop invariant qx == q + j*(q + 2); loop assigns c, j, qx; */ while (j

And the relation c = j % 4 holds initially since 0 = 0 % 4. In the loop body, c becomes (c+1) mod 4, j becomes j+1, and qx becomes qx + q + 2. If these invariants held before an iteration, then after the updates qx' - j'*(q+2) = (qx + q + 2) - (j+1)*(q+2) = qx - j*(q+2) = q, so the first invariant is preserved, and c' = (c+1) mod 4 with j' = j+1 preserves c' = j' % 4. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: I observe the initial state with i9y = 11, yg = 0, oer = 0, and t = 4, which immediately gives yg == oer % 7 at the start. The loop guard oer < i9y restricts oer to stay within the range up to i9y during execution, so 0 <= oer <= i9y holds as the loop progresses. In every iteration, oer is incremented by 1 and yg becomes (yg + 1) mod 7, so after n iterations I have oer = n and yg = n mod 7, yielding yg == oer % 7. Since t is updated by t += yg, after n iterations t equals 4 plus the sum of the first n values of yg, i.e. t = 4 + sum_{i=1..n} (i mod 7). This sum can be written as 4 + 21*(oer/7) + ((oer%7) * ((oer%7)+1))/2, where r = oer%7, using the fact that the block sums are 21 and the partial sum is r(r+1)/2. Therefore the loop invariants arise: i9y == 11, yg == oer % 7, 0 <= oer <= i9y, and t == 4 + 21*(oer/7) + ((oer%7) * ((oer%7)+1))/2, with loop assigns yg, oer, t. int main1(){ int i9y, yg, oer, t; i9y=11; yg=0; oer=0; t=1*4; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant yg == (oer % 7); loop invariant i9y == 11; loop invariant 0 <= oer <= i9y; loop invariant t == 4 + 21*(oer/7) + ((oer%7) * ((oer%7) + 1)) / 2; loop assigns yg, oer, t; */ while (oer

The loop guard oer < i9y ensures that during iterations oer is at most i9y-1, so the quantity i9y - oer is a non-negative integer that will decrease when oer increments. In the body yg is assigned (yg+1)%7 and oer is incremented, so assuming yg == oer % 7 before the iteration I compute new_yg = (old_yg+1)%7 = (old_oer%7+1)%7 = (old_oer+1)%7 = new_oer %7, hence yg == oer %7 is preserved. Finally, t is increased by the updated yg which lies between 0 and 6, so t-4 stays non-negative and increases by at most 6 each loop; thus 0 <= t-4 holds and t-4 <= 6*oer is maintained because t_new-4 = t_old-4 + new_yg <= 6*old_oer + 6 = 6*new_oer. int main1(){ int i9y, yg, oer, t; i9y=11; yg=0; oer=0; t=1*4; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant yg == oer % 7; loop invariant 0 <= t - 4; loop invariant t - 4 <= 6*oer; loop assigns yg, oer, t; loop variant i9y - oer; */ while (oer

chosen: From the initialization, a65 starts at 1 while pz starts at 5 and p36p starts at 0, and kt85 is 23, so the relation pz = 5 + (a65 - 1)^2 holds initially. The loop guard a65 <= kt85 ensures that a65 grows by 1 each iteration, giving the invariant 1 <= a65 <= kt85 + 1. In the body, pz is updated by 2*a65 - 1 before a65 is incremented, so if pz = 5 + (a65 - 1)^2 holds before, then after the update and the increment to a65, pz becomes 5 + (a65' - 1)^2, preserving pz = 5 + (a65 - 1)^2 with a65' = a65 + 1. The body also increases p36p by kt85 and then by i2 + i2 (which is 4), so p36p' = p36p + kt85 + 4; with initial p36p = 0 when a65 = 1, this yields p36p = (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 4), and since kt85 + 4 = kt85 + 2*i2, it also gives p36p = (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2). Thus the loop maintains pz = 5 + (a65 - 1)^2, p36p = (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 4) (equivalently (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2)), and 1 <= a65 <= kt85 + 1. int main1(){ int kt85, kxsd, a65, pz, p36p, i2; kt85=1+22; kxsd=0; a65=1; pz=5; p36p=kxsd; i2=2; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant pz == 5 + (a65 - 1) * (a65 - 1); loop invariant p36p == (kt85 + 4) * (a65 - 1); loop invariant 1 <= a65 <= kt85 + 1; loop invariant p36p == (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2); loop assigns pz, a65, p36p; */ while (1) { if (!(a65<=kt85)) { break; } pz = pz+2*a65-1; a65 = a65 + 1; p36p += kt85; p36p = p36p+i2+i2; } }
rejected: I observe from initialization that a65 is set to 1 and p36p is set to 0, so p36p = (kt85 + 4) * (a65 - 1) holds initially and 1 <= a65 while a65 <= kt85 + 1 holds because kt85 is positive. The loop guard breaks when a65 > kt85, so whenever the body executes I know a65 <= kt85, which implies a65 <= kt85 + 1 at loop entry. Inside the body a65 is incremented by one and p36p is increased by kt85 and then by i2+i2, so the combined update is p36p_new = p36p_old + kt85 + 4. Using the prior equality p36p_old = (kt85 + 4)*(a65_old - 1) I deduce p36p_new = (kt85 + 4)*a65_old = (kt85 + 4)*(a65_new - 1). Because a65 only increases from its initial value 1 I conclude 1 <= a65 is preserved. Because the guard ensures a65_old <= kt85 when the body runs, I conclude a65_new <= kt85 + 1 is preserved, and when the guard fails a65 can be at most kt85 + 1, so this bound holds throughout. int main1(){ int kt85, kxsd, a65, pz, p36p, i2; kt85=1+22; kxsd=0; a65=1; pz=5; p36p=kxsd; i2=2; /*@ loop invariant p36p == (kt85 + 4) * (a65 - 1); loop invariant 1 <= a65; loop invariant a65 <= kt85 + 1; loop assigns pz, a65, p36p; loop variant kt85 - a65; */ while (1) { if (!(a65<=kt85)) { break; } pz = pz+2*a65-1; a65 = a65 + 1; p36p += kt85; p36p = p36p+i2+i2; } }

Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initialization, a65 starts at 1 while pz starts at 5 and p36p starts at 0, and kt85 is 23, so the relation pz = 5 + (a65 - 1)^2 holds initially. The loop guard a65 <= kt85 ensures that a65 grows by 1 each iteration, giving the invariant 1 <= a65 <= kt85 + 1. In the body, pz is updated by 2*a65 - 1 before a65 is incremented, so if pz = 5 + (a65 - 1)^2 holds before, then after the update and the increment to a65, pz becomes 5 + (a65' - 1)^2, preserving pz = 5 + (a65 - 1)^2 with a65' = a65 + 1. The body also increases p36p by kt85 and then by i2 + i2 (which is 4), so p36p' = p36p + kt85 + 4; with initial p36p = 0 when a65 = 1, this yields p36p = (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 4), and since kt85 + 4 = kt85 + 2*i2, it also gives p36p = (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2). Thus the loop maintains pz = 5 + (a65 - 1)^2, p36p = (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 4) (equivalently (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2)), and 1 <= a65 <= kt85 + 1. int main1(){ int kt85, kxsd, a65, pz, p36p, i2; kt85=1+22; kxsd=0; a65=1; pz=5; p36p=kxsd; i2=2; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant pz == 5 + (a65 - 1) * (a65 - 1); loop invariant p36p == (kt85 + 4) * (a65 - 1); loop invariant 1 <= a65 <= kt85 + 1; loop invariant p36p == (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2); loop assigns pz, a65, p36p; */ while (1) { if (!(a65<=kt85)) { break; } pz = pz+2*a65-1; a65 = a65 + 1; p36p += kt85; p36p = p36p+i2+i2; } }
rejected: From initialization, with kt85 = 23, a65 = 1, pz = 5, p36p = 0, and i2 = 2, I see that pz equals 5 + (a65 - 1)^2 and p36p equals (kt85 + 4) * (a65 - 1) and also equals (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2), since both right-hand sides evaluate to 0. During each iteration the loop guard a65 <= kt85 ensures the body executes for a65 values from 1 up to kt85. In the body, pz is updated by 2*a65 - 1 and a65 is incremented by 1, so after the update pz equals 5 + (a65' - 1)^2, preserving pz == 5 + (a65 - 1)^2. The body also updates p36p first by kt85 and then by i2 + i2; since i2 = 2, this totals kt85 + 4, and with a65' = a65 + 1, we obtain p36p' = (kt85 + 4) * (a65' - 1) and also p36p' = (a65' - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2). Therefore the relations pz == 5 + (a65 - 1)^2 and p36p == (kt85 + 4) * (a65 - 1) and p36p == (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2) hold after each iteration. int main1(){ int kt85, kxsd, a65, pz, p36p, i2; kt85=1+22; kxsd=0; a65=1; pz=5; p36p=kxsd; i2=2; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant pz == 5 + (a65 - 1) * (a65 - 1); loop invariant p36p == (kt85 + 4) * (a65 - 1); loop invariant p36p == (a65 - 1) * (kt85 + 2*i2); loop assigns pz, a65, p36p; */ while (1) { if (!(a65<=kt85)) { break; } pz = pz+2*a65-1; a65 = a65 + 1; p36p += kt85; p36p = p36p+i2+i2; } }

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization I have s equal to its initial value s0, xzu = 0, thr = l, pl = 0, and z = l+22. The loop guard ensures the loop only runs when xzu <= z-1, so during the loop xzu stays nonnegative and does not exceed z (the z<0 case is covered by the disjunction). In the loop body, xzu is incremented by 1, thr is increased by 3, pl is advanced by 1 modulo 7, and s is increased by the new xzu value. Therefore after k iterations I obtain xzu = k, thr = l + 3k, pl = k mod 7, and s = s0 + sum_{i=1..k} i = s0 + k(k+1)/2. This yields the invariant s == Pre(s) + xzu*(xzu+1)/2, thr == l + 3*xzu, and pl == xzu % 7. Since z remains equal to l+22, we also have z == \at(l, Pre) + 22, and 0 <= xzu with (z < 0 or xzu <= z) by the guard. int main1(int s,int l){ int z, pl, xzu, thr; z=l+22; pl=0; xzu=0; thr=l; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant s == \at(s, Pre) + xzu*(xzu+1)/2; loop invariant thr == l + 3*xzu; loop invariant pl == xzu % 7; loop invariant 0 <= xzu && (z < 0 || xzu <= z); loop invariant z == \at(l, Pre) + 22; loop invariant z == l + 22; loop assigns s, thr, pl, xzu; */ while (1) { if (!(xzu<=z-1)) { break; } thr = thr + 3; pl = (pl+1)%7; xzu += 1; s = s + xzu; } }
rejected: I observe the initial state with z = l+22, pl = 0, xzu = 0, thr = l, so thr equals l + 3*xzu at the start since xzu is 0. The loop guard xzu <= z-1 uses z = l+22, which keeps xzu in a range where z - xzu is nonnegative and will decrease by 1 whenever xzu increases by 1. In each iteration thr is updated by thr = thr + 3 and xzu is updated by xzu = xzu + 1, so after k iterations thr = l + 3k and xzu = k, yielding thr = l + 3*xzu. Also, pl is updated as pl = (pl+1) % 7 starting from 0, so after k iterations pl = k mod 7, which equals xzu mod 7 since xzu = k; hence pl == xzu % 7. Since xzu starts at 0 and only increases, 0 <= xzu is preserved. Therefore, at loop heads thr == l + 3*xzu, pl == xzu % 7, 0 <= xzu, and the variant z - xzu nonincreases and decreases by 1 each iteration. int main1(int s,int l){ int z, pl, xzu, thr; z=l+22; pl=0; xzu=0; thr=l; /*@ loop invariant thr == l + 3*xzu; loop invariant pl == xzu % 7; loop invariant 0 <= xzu; loop variant z - xzu; loop assigns s, thr, pl, xzu; @*/ while (1) { if (!(xzu<=z-1)) { break; } thr = thr + 3; pl = (pl+1)%7; xzu += 1; s = s + xzu; } }

Pl starts at 0 and is updated as (pl+1) mod 7, so its value always stays between 0 and 6. Xzu starts at 0 and increases by 1 each iteration, and the loop guard xzu <= z-1 ensures 0 <= xzu and, when z >= 0, xzu <= z (or trivially true if z < 0). Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, xmt is 10 and cpd, zbp, tgk are 0; thus tgk = cpd*(xmt+1) holds (0 = 0*11), zbp = cpd mod 7 holds (0 = 0 mod 7), and 0 <= cpd <= xmt holds (0 <= 0 <= 10). The loop guard cpd < xmt ensures the loop can run only while cpd stays in [0, xmt], and during each iteration cpd is incremented by 1, so the bound 0 <= cpd <= xmt remains true after each iteration. In the loop body, tgk is increased by xmt and then by 1, so tgk increases by xmt+1 per iteration; with cpd increasing by 1, tgk' = tgk + xmt + 1 and cpd' = cpd + 1 imply tgk' = cpd'*(xmt+1). Simultaneously, zbp becomes (zbp+1) mod 7 while cpd becomes cpd+1, so zbp' = (cpd+1) mod 7, i.e., zbp' = cpd' mod 7, preserving the second invariant. int main1(){ int xmt, d5e, zbp, cpd, tgk; xmt=(1%40)+9; d5e=0; zbp=0; cpd=0; tgk=d5e; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant tgk == cpd * (xmt + 1); loop invariant zbp == cpd % 7; loop invariant 0 <= cpd <= xmt; loop assigns cpd, tgk, zbp; */ while (cpd
rejected: From initialization I note that cpd starts at 0 and zbp starts at 0, while the loop guard uses cpd < xmt. In the loop body, cpd is incremented by 1 and zbp is updated as (zbp+1) mod 7, with tgk also updated, so the two variables cpd and zbp change in a tightly linked, regular way each iteration. After k iterations, cpd equals k and zbp equals k mod 7, so the relation zbp == cpd mod 7 emerges. Because the loop starts at cpd = 0 and continues only while cpd < xmt, cpd remains within 0 to xmt, yielding 0 <= cpd <= xmt. The loop assigns cpd, tgk, and zbp, so these variables are the ones whose relationships appear in the invariant conclusions. Consequently, the derived invariant relationships are zbp == cpd % 7 and 0 <= cpd <= xmt. int main1(){ int xmt, d5e, zbp, cpd, tgk; xmt=(1%40)+9; d5e=0; zbp=0; cpd=0; tgk=d5e; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant zbp == cpd % 7; loop invariant 0 <= cpd <= xmt; loop assigns cpd, tgk, zbp; */ while (cpd

chosen: I observe the initial state c=12, mp=0, y=0, eymz=-6, and from that I deduce 0 <= y and y <= c, plus mp = y % 6 and eymz = -6 + y(y+1)/2. The loop guard y < c guarantees that at the start of every iteration y <= c-1, hence y <= c holds throughout the loop body. In the loop body I compute mp' = (mp+1) mod 6 and y' = y+1, so if mp = y mod 6 before, then mp' = (y+1) mod 6, i.e. mp' = y' mod 6. Also eymz' = eymz + y', and substituting eymz = -6 + y(y+1)/2 gives eymz' = -6 + y(y+1)/2 + (y+1) = -6 + y'(y'+1)/2. Therefore after the iteration the relations 0 <= y, y <= c, mp == y % 6, and eymz == -6 + y(y+1)/2 remain true with y replaced by y'. The statement y++ increases y by one from a nonnegative value, so 0 <= y and y <= c remain true while the loop runs. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: I observe the loop starts with iza = 1, s0qi = 0, zvry = 0, and x = 0, so the initial state ties x and zvry to zero and fixes the parameters iza and s0qi. The loop guard x < iza will permit execution only while x is 0, and since iza is 1, the loop will perform at most one iteration, keeping 0 <= x <= iza throughout. Inside the body, x increases by 1 and zvry increases by 1 modulo 4, while the conditional branch never fires because s0qi + 5 <= s0qi + iza is false for the given values, so zvry is updated only by (zvry+1) % 4. Starting from zvry = 0 and x = 0, after one iteration we have x = 1 and zvry = 1, which matches zvry = x % 4. Since iza and s0qi are not modified inside the loop, they remain iza = 1 and s0qi = 0 for all iterations. Thus the invariants reflect that iza stays 1, s0qi stays 0, 0 <= x <= iza, and zvry tracks x modulo 4, with the loop able to update only zvry and x. int main1(){ int iza, s0qi, zvry, x; iza=1; s0qi=0; zvry=0; x=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant zvry == x % 4; loop invariant iza == 1; loop invariant s0qi == 0; loop invariant 0 <= x <= iza; loop assigns zvry, x; */ while (x
rejected: I initialize iza to 1 and s0qi to 0 before entering the loop, so those equalities hold at loop entry. I note that neither iza nor s0qi is assigned inside the loop body, therefore those values remain constant and the equalities are preserved as invariants. With iza == 1 and s0qi == 0 the inequality s0qi + 5 <= s0qi + iza simplifies to 5 <= 1 which is false, so its negation holds initially and continues to hold since s0qi and iza do not change. The loop guard x < iza with initial x == 0 and iza == 1 gives a nonnegative difference iza - x, and because each iteration increments x by one, iza - x strictly decreases by one each loop while remaining nonnegative, yielding the loop variant (iza - x). Observing the loop body shows only assignments to x and zvry, so the only variables that can change during the loop are x and zvry. int main1(){ int iza, s0qi, zvry, x; iza=1; s0qi=0; zvry=0; x=0; /*@ loop invariant iza == 1; loop invariant s0qi == 0; loop invariant !(s0qi + 5 <= s0qi + iza); loop assigns x, zvry; loop variant (iza - x); */ while (x

rejected: From the pre-loop state, iza is a fixed bound and the code path preserves iza < 5, so this bound remains true throughout the loop. The if condition reduces to 5 <= iza, which, given iza < 5, is false, so the doubling branch zvry = zvry + zvry is never taken inside the loop. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization I see ll = 3 * Pre(e), y = 0, cotj = 0, lu = 0, and t equal to its initial value; therefore I derive lu == cotj % 3, y == 0, ll == 3 * Pre(e), and t == Pre(t) + cotj * (y + 1) at the starting point. The loop guard ensures we only execute while cotj <= ll - 1, which implies cotj will stay within 0 and ll when ll is nonnegative, and a separate branch covers ll < 0. In the loop body, cotj is incremented by 1, lu becomes (lu + 1) % 3, and t is increased by y and then by 1, so t increases by y + 1 per iteration. Thus lu progresses in lockstep with cotj, preserving lu == cotj % 3 after each iteration. Since cotj counts iterations and t is increased by y + 1 each time, the relation t == Pre(t) + cotj * (y + 1) is preserved. The invariant y == 0 remains true because y is never modified inside the loop. Finally, the bound (ll < 0) || (0 <= cotj && cotj <= ll) is maintained since cotj starts at 0 and cannot exceed ll while the loop condition allows continuation, unless ll is negative, in which case the first part holds. int main1(int t,int e){ int ll, y, lu, cotj; ll=e*3; y=0; lu=0; cotj=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant lu == cotj % 3; loop invariant t == \at(t, Pre) + cotj * (y + 1); loop invariant ll == 3 * \at(e,Pre); loop invariant y == 0; loop invariant (ll < 0) || (0 <= cotj && cotj <= ll); loop invariant 0 <= cotj; loop assigns cotj, t, lu; */ while (1) { if (!(cotj<=ll-1)) { break; } cotj = cotj + 1; t = t + y; lu = (lu+1)%3; t += 1; } }
rejected: At loop entry, cotj is initialized to 0 and t is initialized to Pre(t), so the base case t = Pre(t) holds when cotj = 0. Each iteration increases cotj by 1 and increases t by y, then by 1, so t increases by y+1 for every increment of cotj. Therefore the relation t = Pre(t) + cotj*(y+1) holds after any number of iterations, since the constant Pre(t) is preserved and the accumulated increments are captured by cotj*(y+1). Since cotj starts nonnegative and is only increased, cotj >= 0 throughout the loop. The loop guard continues while cotj <= ll-1, so cotj cannot reach ll and the quantity ll - cotj decreases with each iteration, providing ll - cotj as a loop variant. Lu is updated in the body but the invariant relating t and cotj does not depend on lu, so the invariant remains focused on cotj, t, and Pre(t). /*@ loop invariant cotj >= 0; loop invariant t == \at(t, Pre) + cotj * (y + 1); loop assigns cotj, t, lu; loop variant ll - cotj; */ while (1) { if (!(cotj<=ll-1)) { break; } cotj = cotj + 1; t = t + y; lu = (lu+1)%3; t += 1; }

Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, o is set to 2*d, zi1 is 0, and w is 0 with d equal to its pre-loop value, so o = 2 * \at(d,Pre) and d = \at(d,Pre) when zi1 = 0, i.e., d = \at(d,Pre) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2 at this point. Since zi1 starts at 0 and only increases by 1 each iteration, I conclude 0 <= zi1 holds at loop entry and is maintained. The loop updates w to (w+1) mod 8 and then increments zi1 by 1, so after any number of iterations w equals zi1 mod 8, hence w == zi1 % 8. In the body, d is increased by the new zi1 value, so d' = d + zi1' where zi1' = zi1 + 1. Substituting before the iteration gives d' = \at(d,Pre) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2 + (zi1+1) = \at(d,Pre) + zi1'*(zi1'+1)/2, thus the invariant d == \at(d,Pre) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2 is preserved. Since o remains equal to 2 * \at(d,Pre), and the equalities w == zi1 % 8 and 0 <= zi1 hold, all three updated variables satisfy the loop invariants, and the loop assigns d, w, zi1 as stated. int main1(int d){ int o, w, zi1; o=d*2; w=0; zi1=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant d == (\at(d, Pre) + (zi1*(zi1+1))/2); loop invariant o == (2 * \at(d, Pre)); loop invariant w == (zi1 % 8); loop invariant 0 <= zi1; loop assigns d, w, zi1; */ while (zi1<=o-1) { w = (w+1)%8; zi1 = zi1 + 1; d += zi1; } }
rejected: From the initialization o = d*2, w = 0, zi1 = 0, I see zi1 starts at 0 and w starts at 0, and o is defined from the initial d. Because zi1 starts at 0 and the loop guard requires zi1 <= o-1, zi1 remains in the range 0 to o during the loop. Each iteration updates w with (w+1) mod 8, so w stays within 0 to 7. Each iteration increments zi1 by 1, so zi1 takes values 1, 2, ..., up to o, keeping 0 <= zi1 <= o. The update to d adds the new zi1 value, so after zi1 iterations d equals the initial d plus the sum 1 through zi1, i.e., Pre + zi1*(zi1+1)/2. The loop variant o - zi1 decreases by 1 each iteration since zi1 increases by 1, and termination occurs when zi1 reaches o. int main1(int d){ int o, w, zi1; o=d*2; w=0; zi1=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 0 <= zi1 <= o; loop invariant 0 <= w && w < 8; loop invariant d == \at(d,Pre) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2; loop assigns d, w, zi1; loop variant o - zi1; */ while (zi1<=o-1) { w = (w+1)%8; zi1 = zi1 + 1; d += zi1; } }

Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, o is set to 2*d, zi1 is 0, and w is 0 with d equal to its pre-loop value, so o = 2 * \at(d,Pre) and d = \at(d,Pre) when zi1 = 0, i.e., d = \at(d,Pre) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2 at this point. Since zi1 starts at 0 and only increases by 1 each iteration, I conclude 0 <= zi1 holds at loop entry and is maintained. The loop updates w to (w+1) mod 8 and then increments zi1 by 1, so after any number of iterations w equals zi1 mod 8, hence w == zi1 % 8. In the body, d is increased by the new zi1 value, so d' = d + zi1' where zi1' = zi1 + 1. Substituting before the iteration gives d' = \at(d,Pre) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2 + (zi1+1) = \at(d,Pre) + zi1'*(zi1'+1)/2, thus the invariant d == \at(d,Pre) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2 is preserved. Since o remains equal to 2 * \at(d,Pre), and the equalities w == zi1 % 8 and 0 <= zi1 hold, all three updated variables satisfy the loop invariants, and the loop assigns d, w, zi1 as stated. int main1(int d){ int o, w, zi1; o=d*2; w=0; zi1=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant d == (\at(d, Pre) + (zi1*(zi1+1))/2); loop invariant o == (2 * \at(d, Pre)); loop invariant w == (zi1 % 8); loop invariant 0 <= zi1; loop assigns d, w, zi1; */ while (zi1<=o-1) { w = (w+1)%8; zi1 = zi1 + 1; d += zi1; } }
rejected: From initialization, I observe o is set to 2 times the original d and zi1 is 0, so the current d equals the original d, which matches d == Pre(d) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2 when zi1 = 0. Since o is never updated inside the loop, it remains equal to 2 * Pre(d) throughout, yielding o == 2 * Pre(d). The loop guard zi1 <= o-1 implies that inside the loop zi1 cannot exceed o-1, hence zi1 <= o holds as an invariant. In each iteration, zi1 is incremented by 1 and d is increased by this new zi1, so after k iterations the total added to d is 1+2+...+k = k*(k+1)/2, giving d = Pre(d) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2. Therefore the relation o == 2 * Pre(d) remains true after updates, reinforcing the O-invariant alongside the D-invariant. Consequently, at any loop point, the invariants d == Pre(d) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2, o == 2 * Pre(d), and zi1 <= o are established by initialization and the deterministic body updates. int main1(int d){ int o, w, zi1; o=d*2; w=0; zi1=0; /*@ loop invariant d == \at(d, Pre) + zi1*(zi1+1)/2; loop invariant o == 2 * \at(d, Pre); loop invariant zi1 <= o; loop assigns d, w, zi1; loop variant o - zi1; */ while (zi1<=o-1) { w = (w+1)%8; zi1 = zi1 + 1; d += zi1; } }

Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: I observe that at entry a is set to the initial c, s2 is 1, and sl is 0, so a equals the pre-state of c and sl starts at 0. From this I deduce that initially c = Pre(c) + sl and e = Pre(e) + 0, i.e., e = Pre(e) when sl = 0. In each iteration the loop doubles s2, increments sl by 1, increments c by 1, and increments e by the new sl, so sl becomes sl' = sl + 1 and e becomes e + sl'. Consequently the relation c = Pre(c) + sl is preserved across iterations since c' = c + 1 and sl' = sl + 1. Also e' = e + sl' implies e' = Pre(e) + sl'(sl'+1)/2, preserving e = Pre(e) + sl*(sl+1)/2 with sl updated to sl'. Sl remains nonnegative because it starts at 0 and only increases, and s2 stays at least 1 because it starts at 1 and is always doubled. int main1(int c,int e){ int a, s2, sl; a=c; s2=1; sl=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant a == \at(c,Pre); loop invariant c == \at(c,Pre) + sl; loop invariant e == \at(e,Pre) + sl * (sl + 1) / 2; loop invariant sl >= 0; loop invariant s2 >= 1; loop assigns s2, sl, c, e; */ while (s2<=a) { s2 = 2*s2; sl++; c = (1)+(c); e = e + sl; } }
rejected: I observe that a is set to the initial value of c, while s2 is initialized to 1 and sl to 0 at the start. The loop guard s2 <= a ties the number of iterations to a and ensures s2 grows by doubling while the condition holds. In the loop body, s2 becomes 2*s2, sl is incremented by 1, c is incremented by 1, and e is incremented by sl. Therefore after k iterations, sl equals k, c equals Pre(c) plus k, and s2 equals 2^k. From this, c equals Pre(c) plus sl, i.e., c == \at(c,Pre) + sl. Since sl starts at 0 and only increases, sl >= 0. Because the loop runs only while s2 <= a and s2 doubles each iteration, a - s2 remains non-negative and strictly decreases with every iteration, providing a decreasing variant. int main1(int c,int e){ int a, s2, sl; a=c; s2=1; sl=0; /*@ loop invariant a == \at(c,Pre); loop invariant c == \at(c,Pre) + sl; loop invariant sl >= 0; loop assigns s2, sl, c, e; loop variant a - s2; */ while (s2<=a) { s2 = 2*s2; sl++; c = (1)+(c); e = e + sl; } }

Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization I see that a is set to ca and ca equals 14, so a == ca; t0 starts at 1 and v starts at 0, giving 0 <= v and t0 >= 1; and f initially equals its Pre value, which with v = 0 yields f == \at(f, Pre) + a * v. The loop guard uses t0 <= ca, which preserves the relevance of these relations since t0 remains at least 1 and a stays equal to ca at loop entry. In the loop body, f is updated by f = f + a, v is incremented by 1, and t0 is doubled, so after one iteration f increases by a, v increases by 1, and t0 becomes 2*t0. If before an iteration f == \at(f, Pre) + a * v held, then after updating to v' = v + 1 and f' = f + a we have f' = \at(f, Pre) + a * v', so the invariant f == \at(f, Pre) + a * v continues to hold. Meanwhile a remains equal to ca and v only grows from 0 upward while t0 only grows from 1 upward (doubling), so 0 <= v, a == ca, and t0 >= 1 persist. int main1(int f){ int ca, a, t0, v; ca=14; a=ca; t0=1; v=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant f == \at(f, Pre) + a * v; loop invariant 0 <= v; loop invariant t0 >= 1; loop invariant a == ca; loop assigns f, v, t0; */ while (1) { if (!(t0<=ca)) { break; } f = f + a; v = (1)+(v); t0 = 2*t0; } }
rejected: I observe that the initialization fixes a = 14, t0 = 1, and v = 0, so at loop entry f = Pre(f) and v*a = 0, yielding f = Pre(f) + v*a. The loop continues while t0 <= ca; since ca = 14 and t0 starts at 1 and is doubled each iteration, t0 remains at least 1 and the loop stops once t0 > ca. In each iteration, f is increased by a, v is incremented by 1, and t0 is multiplied by 2. Hence after k iterations, v = k, t0 = 2^k, and f = Pre(f) + k*a, i.e., f = Pre(f) + v*a. Since t0 = 2^v, i.e., t0 = (1 << v), and the body updates f, v, t0, the loop assigns f, v, t0. int main1(int f){ int ca, a, t0, v; ca=14; a=ca; t0=1; v=0; /*@ loop invariant t0 >= 1; loop invariant f == \at(f, Pre) + v * a; loop invariant t0 == (1 << v); loop assigns f, v, t0; */ while (1) { if (!(t0<=ca)) { break; } f = f + a; v = (1)+(v); t0 = 2*t0; } }

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, aik6 equals 3, gn and ke start at 0, and v starts at 8. The loop guard gn < aik6 restricts gn to the values 0, 1, 2 during the loop. Each iteration increments gn by 1 and increases v by aik6, so after any number of iterations v becomes 8 plus gn times aik6 at loop points. The body increments ke by 4 exactly when gn >= aik6/2, i.e., once gn reaches 1, so ke remains 0 for gn = 0 or 1 and becomes 4 when gn = 2, matching ke = 4*(gn - aik6/2) for gn above aik6/2. Therefore, I derive that 0 <= gn <= aik6, v = 8 + gn*aik6, and ke follows the piecewise relation determined by gn relative to aik6/2. int main1(){ int aik6, mk, gn, ke, v; aik6=1*3; mk=0; gn=0; ke=mk; v=8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 0 <= gn; loop invariant gn <= aik6; loop invariant v == 8 + gn * aik6; loop invariant ((gn <= (aik6/2)) && ke == 0) || ((gn > (aik6/2)) && ke == 4 * (gn - (aik6/2))); loop assigns gn, ke, v; */ while (gn=aik6/2))) { ke += 4; } gn = gn + 1; v = v + aik6; } }
rejected: From my initialization, aik6 is set to 3, gn is 0, ke is 0, and v is 8, so I observe the relation v = 8 + aik6 * gn at loop entry. In every iteration I increment gn by 1 and increase v by aik6 (which is constant), so after k iterations gn = k and v = 8 + aik6 * k, preserving the relation v = 8 + aik6 * gn. I note that ke is updated by 4 only when gn >= aik6/2, so the increments of ke occur exactly when gn crosses the threshold. Since aik6/2 evaluates to 1 with integer division, I separate two regimes: gn < 1 and gn >= 1. Therefore I derive that ke remains 0 while gn < aik6/2, and once gn >= aik6/2, ke equals 4 * (gn - aik6/2), i.e., ke = 4*(gn - aik6/2) at loop heads. int main1(){ int aik6, mk, gn, ke, v; aik6=1*3; mk=0; gn=0; ke=mk; v=8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant v == 8 + aik6 * gn; loop invariant (gn < aik6/2) ==> (ke == 0); = aik6/2) ==> (ke == 4 * (gn - aik6/2)); loop assigns gn, v, ke; loop variant aik6 - gn; */ while (gn=aik6/2))) { ke += 4; } gn = gn + 1; v = v + aik6; } }

Finally, the loop variant aik6 - gn decreases by 1 each iteration because gn increases by 1, starting from 3 and reaching aik6 when the loop terminates. int main1(){ int aik6, mk, gn, ke, v; aik6=1*3; mk=0; gn=0; ke=mk; v=8; /*@ loop invariant 0 <= gn && gn <= aik6; loop invariant v == 8 + gn * aik6; loop assigns gn, ke, v; loop variant (aik6 - gn); */ while (gn=aik6/2))) { ke += 4; } gn = gn + 1; v = v + aik6; } }

chosen: From initialization, j starts at 0, i starts at 0, kdi starts at 0, and jsgp remains the fixed value 18, so the basic bounds 0 <= j and kdi >= 0 hold at the loop entry and j is bounded above by jsgp. The loop guard enforces that j increases by one each iteration and never reaches jsgp, so across the loop execution we maintain 0 <= j <= jsgp. Inside the loop, i is incremented only when j >= jsgp/2, hence before reaching the halfway point i stays at 0, giving the implication that j < jsgp/2 implies i == 0. Once j passes the halfway point, i accumulates in steps of 4, yielding i == 4*(j - jsgp/2) for j >= jsgp/2. The variable kdi is updated by adding i each iteration starting from kdi = 0, so kdi remains nonnegative and, during the first half of the loop when i is 0, kdi stays 0, giving (j <= jsgp/2) ==> kdi == 0. Since the loop only assigns to i, j, and kdi, their tracked relationships fully reflect the progression of the loop body. int main1(){ int jsgp, j, i, kdi; jsgp=18; j=0; i=0; kdi=0; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 0 <= j; loop invariant j <= jsgp; loop invariant (j <= jsgp/2) ==> (kdi == 0); loop invariant kdi >= 0; loop invariant (j < jsgp/2 ==> i == 0) && (j >= jsgp/2 ==> i == 4 * (j - jsgp/2)); loop assigns i, j, kdi; */ while (j=jsgp/2) { i += 4; } kdi = kdi + i; j++; } }
rejected: I start from initialization where jsgp = 18, j = 0, i = 0, and kdi = 0, so at the loop head for j up to jsgp/2 the values of i and kdi are still 0. The loop guard ensures j advances from 0 to 17 (while j < jsgp) and it increments by 1 each iteration, giving a natural decreasing loop variant of jsgp - j. The only assignment to i is i += 4 and it occurs only when j >= jsgp/2, so for all iterations with j <= jsgp/2 i remains 0. When j exceeds the halfway point, each iteration increases i by 4 and since j grows by 1 per iteration, at the start of an iteration with j >= jsgp/2 we have i = 4*(j - jsgp/2). The update kdi = kdi + i uses the current i, so while i is 0 (for j <= jsgp/2) kdi stays 0. Therefore, the observed relationships are: if j <= jsgp/2 then i = 0 and kdi = 0, and if j >= jsgp/2 then i = 4*(j - jsgp/2). int main1(){ int jsgp, j, i, kdi; jsgp=18; j=0; i=0; kdi=0; /*@ loop invariant (j <= jsgp/2) ==> (i == 0); loop invariant (j >= jsgp/2) ==> (i == 4 * (j - jsgp/2)); loop invariant (j <= jsgp/2) ==> (kdi == 0); loop assigns i, j, kdi; loop variant (jsgp - j); */ while (j=jsgp/2) { i += 4; } kdi = kdi + i; j++; } }

instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization I have n = 75, wc6 = 0, and x = v, so at the loop start p equals its pre-state (with wc6 = 0) and n is 75. Because n is fixed, I deduce n remains 75 throughout the loop. Each iteration increments p by n and increments wc6 by 1, so after wc6 iterations p = Pre(p) + wc6 * n. The loop guard wc6 <= n-1 keeps wc6 within 0 to n during the run, hence 0 <= wc6 <= n. The conditional x += 4 runs exactly when wc6 >= n/2, otherwise x stays unchanged, so for wc6 < n/2 we have x = v, and for wc6 >= n/2 we have x = v + 4*(wc6 - n/2). Therefore the relations p == Pre(p) + wc6 * n, 0 <= wc6 <= n, n == 75, (wc6 < n/2) ==> (x == v), and (wc6 >= n/2) ==> (x == v + 4*(wc6 - n/2)) arise from the observed initialization, guard, and body updates. int main1(int p,int v){ int n, wc6, x; n=75; wc6=0; x=v; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant p == \at(p, Pre) + wc6 * n; loop invariant 0 <= wc6 <= n; loop invariant n == 75; loop invariant (wc6 < n/2) ==> (x == v); loop invariant (wc6 >= n/2) ==> (x == v + 4*(wc6 - n/2)); loop assigns x, wc6, p; */ while (wc6<=n-1) { if (!(!(wc6>=n/2))) { x += 4; } wc6 += 1; p += n; } }
rejected: I observe that wc6 is initialized to 0 and x is set to v, establishing the starting values for p, wc6, and x. I note that in every iteration wc6 is incremented by 1 and p is increased by n, so after k iterations p equals the original p plus k*n. From this, I derive that p == \at(p, Pre) + wc6 * n holds throughout the loop, since the total p increment is exactly wc6 times n. I see that x is updated by 4 only when wc6 >= n/2, so once wc6 reaches at least n/2, x equals v plus 4 for each subsequent iteration, i.e., x == v + 4*(wc6 - n/2). Because wc6 increases by 1 each iteration and the loop continues while wc6 <= n-1, the quantity n - wc6 decreases by 1 each time, providing a loop variant that ensures termination. int main1(int p,int v){ int n, wc6, x; n=75; wc6=0; x=v; /*@ loop invariant (wc6 >= n/2) ==> (x == v + 4*(wc6 - n/2)); loop invariant p == \at(p, Pre) + wc6 * n; loop assigns p, wc6, x; loop variant n - wc6; */ while (wc6<=n-1) { if (!(!(wc6>=n/2))) { x += 4; } wc6 += 1; p += n; } }

The loop guard ensures wc6 remains in 0..n-1 during the loop, and after each iteration wc6 is incremented by 1, so 0 <= wc6 <= n holds at loop entry and remains true across iterations. If wc6 < n/2, x remains v and after the increment wc6' may still be below n/2 or reach n/2; in either case the appropriate branch of the invariant holds for wc6', ensuring the invariant relationships persist into the next iteration. int main1(int p,int v){ int n, wc6, x; n=75; wc6=0; x=v; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 0 <= wc6 <= n; loop invariant (wc6 < n/2) ==> (x == v); loop invariant (wc6 >= n/2) ==> (x == v + 4*(wc6 - n/2)); loop assigns x, wc6, p; */ while (wc6<=n-1) { if (!(!(wc6>=n/2))) { x += 4; } wc6 += 1; p += n; } }

Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: At loop start, k93 is set to the initial value of r, kr is 0, j is 3, and l holds its initial value, establishing the base facts. Since r is never modified inside the loop, k93 remains equal to the original r throughout the loop. Each iteration increments kr by 1 and then adds the new kr to l, so after t iterations l has increased by the sum 1+2+...+t, i.e., t(t+1)/2 beyond its initial value. Therefore, at any point l equals its initial value plus kr(kr+1)/2, since kr equals the number of iterations executed so far. Because kr starts at 0 and only increases, kr is always >= 0. Because j starts at 3 and may only be increased, j is always >= 3. Since k93 stays tied to the pre-state of r, k93 remains equal to the original r value throughout the loop. int main1(int l,int r){ int k93, kr, j; k93=r; kr=0; j=3; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant l == \at(l,Pre) + kr * (kr + 1) / 2; loop invariant k93 == \at(r,Pre); loop invariant kr >= 0; loop invariant j >= 3; loop assigns j, kr, l; */ while (kr=k93/2) { j += 2; } kr += 1; l = l + kr; } }
rejected: From the initialization k93 = r and kr = 0, I see that kr starts at 0 and will be increased by 1 on each iteration until it reaches k93. The loop guard kr < k93 ensures kr never exceeds k93, so 0 <= kr <= k93 holds at the start of every iteration. In the body, after kr is incremented, l is updated by adding the new value of kr, so over the first kr iterations l accumulates 1 + 2 + ... + kr. That sum equals kr*(kr+1)/2, hence l equals its pre-loop value plus kr*(kr+1)/2. Since kr grows by 1 each iteration, the quantity k93 - kr decreases by 1, giving a strictly decreasing loop variant. Collecting these, I derive that l equals the pre-loop l plus kr*(kr+1)/2, kr remains within [0, k93], and the loop variant is k93 - kr. int main1(int l,int r){ int k93, kr, j; k93=r; kr=0; j=3; /*@ loop invariant l == \at(l,Pre) + kr * (kr + 1) / 2; loop invariant 0 <= kr <= k93; loop assigns j, kr, l; loop variant k93 - kr; */ while (kr<k93) { if (kr>=k93/2) { j += 2; } kr += 1; l = l + kr; } }
