Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: I initialize vy to 2, l12 to 0, y to 11, fe to 0, and hs to vy, so vy=2, hs=2, fe=0, l12=0, y=11. The loop guard uses l12 < vy, so with vy=2 the loop runs for l12 values 0 and 1, keeping 0 <= l12 < vy. In the loop body, l12 is incremented by 1, and fe is updated to hs only if hs <= fe; since hs=2 and fe=0, this condition is false and fe remains 0. Because fe stays 0 and l12 increases, the update y = y + l12 - fe adds exactly the current l12 to y. Therefore, after t iterations, y equals 11 plus the sum of 1 through t, i.e., 11 + t*(t+1)/2, which matches y == 11 + (l12*(l12+1))/2 - l12*fe. Also since hs was set to vy and never changed, hs == vy and vy == 2; together with fe == 0 and 0 <= l12 <= vy, these conditions describe the loop invariants, and the loop mutates only l12, fe, and y. int main1(){ int vy, c73, l12, y, fe, hs; vy=1*2; c73=0; l12=0; y=11; fe=c73; hs=vy; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant vy == 2; loop invariant hs == vy; loop invariant fe == 0; loop invariant y == 11 + (l12*(l12+1))/2 - l12*fe; loop invariant 0 <= l12; loop invariant l12 <= vy; loop assigns l12, fe, y; */ while (1) { if (!(l12
rejected: I observe that vy is not reassigned in the loop body, and the pre-loop assignment fixes vy to 2, so vy remains 2 on every iteration, yielding vy == 2. I observe that hs is not modified in the loop body, so its value stays fixed; if the loop entry satisfies hs == vy, then hs == vy persists throughout the loop. The loop continues only while l12 < vy, and inside the body l12 is increased by 1, so l12 never becomes negative and never exceeds vy, giving 0 <= l12 <= vy. The body directly assigns l12, fe, and y, so these three variables are indeed updated by the loop. Since vy is constant and l12 increases by 1 each iteration, the difference vy - l12 decreases by 1 each time and remains nonnegative while the loop guard holds, providing the loop variant vy - l12. /*@ loop invariant vy == 2; loop invariant hs == vy; loop invariant 0 <= l12 & l12 <= vy; loop assigns l12, fe, y; loop variant vy - l12; */ while (1) { if (!(l12<vy)) { break; } l12 = l12 + 1; if (hs<=fe) { fe = hs; } y = y+l12-fe; }

Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization I know of equals 26, aa6 equals 0, uo equals 26, and og equals 6, which gives og == 6, of == 26, aa6 is 0 and thus even. The loop guard aa6 <= of - 1 means aa6 is at most 25 before each iteration, so across iterations aa6 stays in the 0..26 range as it increases by 2. Each iteration increments aa6 by 2, preserving aa6 % 2 == 0 and ensuring 0 <= aa6 <= of as the sequence reaches 26. In the body the condition og <= uo triggers on the first iteration (since 6 <= 26) and sets uo = og, making uo equal to 6. The update og = og + uo - uo simplifies to og, so once uo becomes 6, og remains 6 for all subsequent iterations. With og staying 6 and uo settling at 6 after the first iteration, the relation og <= uo persists, keeping uo at 6 thereafter (or at 26 before the first update). Therefore I derive the loop invariants: og == 6; of == 26; 0 <= aa6 <= of and aa6 % 2 == 0; (uo == 26 || uo == 6). int main1(){ int of, aa6, uo, og; of=26; aa6=0; uo=of; og=6; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant og == 6; loop invariant 0 <= aa6 && aa6 <= of && aa6 % 2 == 0 && (uo == 26 || uo == 6); loop invariant of == 26; loop assigns aa6, uo, og; */ while (aa6<=of-1) { aa6 += 2; if (!(!(og<=uo))) { uo = og; } og = og+uo-uo; } }
rejected: I observe that initially aa6 = 0, so aa6 % 2 == 0, and uo = of = 26 with og = 6, giving uo >= og. Since 26 <= 6 is false, the condition (uo <= og) ==> (uo == og) is vacuously true at the start. In the loop, aa6 is increased by 2, so if aa6 was even before, it remains even after the increment, preserving aa6 % 2 == 0. The conditional reduces to if (og <= uo) then uo = og; given the invariant uo >= og, this branch can fire exactly when og <= uo, and it then sets uo to og, yielding uo == og and thus ensuring that (uo <= og) implies equality. The statement og = og + uo - uo leaves og unchanged, so the relative ordering between uo and og is preserved aside from the potential equality when the branch fires. The loop variant of (of - aa6) starts at 26 and decreases by 2 each iteration, providing a well-founded descent toward termination. int main1(){ int of, aa6, uo, og; of=26; aa6=0; uo=of; og=6; /*@ loop invariant aa6 % 2 == 0; loop invariant uo >= og; loop invariant (uo <= og) ==> (uo == og); loop assigns aa6, uo, og; loop variant of (of - aa6); */ while (aa6<=of-1) { aa6 += 2; if (!(!(og<=uo))) { uo = og; } og = og+uo-uo; } }

chosen: From the initialization, awic becomes 11, d8 becomes 11, rb becomes 11, and m becomes 9; thus the starting triple (d8, rb, m) sums to 31 and all three are non-negative, with d8 equal to awic. Therefore the total sum d8 + rb + m does not change across iterations. Because awic is constant and d8 only increases, the inequality d8 >= awic is preserved. If d8 is even, I update rb = rb - 1; since rb > 0 at that moment, the new rb is still >= 0. If d8 is odd, I update m -= 1; with m > 0 before the update, the new m is >= 0. Therefore after k iterations I have r = -4 + k and s5 + bbq = ((j%20)+10) + ((j%15)+8) - k. This implies s5 + bbq + r + 4 stays equal to ((j%20)+10) + ((j%15)+8). Equivalently, s5 + bbq = (j%20)+(j%15) + 14 - r. Since r starts at -4 and only increases, I deduce r >= -4. And because s5 and bbq only decrease from their initial values, I conclude s5 <= (j%20)+10 and bbq <= (j%15)+8, with the Pre-state use as (\at(j, Pre)). int main1(int j,int w){ int r, s5, bbq; r=-4; s5=(j%20)+10; bbq=(j%15)+8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant s5 + bbq + r + 4 == (j % 20) + 10 + (j % 15) + 8; loop invariant s5 + bbq == (j % 20) + (j % 15) + 14 - r; loop invariant r >= -4; loop invariant s5 + bbq == (\at(j, Pre)%20) + (\at(j, Pre)%15) + 14 - r; loop invariant s5 <= ((\at(j, Pre) % 20) + 10); loop invariant bbq <= ((\at(j, Pre) % 15) + 8); loop assigns s5, bbq, r; */ for (; s5>0&&bbq>0; r = r + 1) { if (r%2==0) { s5--; } else { bbq--; } } }

In each iteration, r becomes r + 1 and we decrement either s5 (when the old r is even) or bbq (when the old r is odd), so exactly one of s5 or bbq decreases by 1 while r increases by 1. Consequently, s5 + bbq decreases by 1 and r increases by 1, keeping the total s5 + bbq + r constant across iterations. Since the initial constant is (j%20) + (j%15) + 14, we have s5 + bbq + r = (j%20) + (j%15) + 14, which is equivalent to s5 + bbq + r + 4 = (j%20) + 10 + (j%15) + 8. instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: At initialization, k starts at 0 and i and qv start at (a%20)+10 and (a%15)+8, so k is non-negative and i, qv are bounded by those initial values. In each iteration, if k is even we decrement i, otherwise we decrement qv, and then k is incremented by 1, which preserves the overall accounting of the variables. Consequently, the sum i + qv + k remains equal to the initial value (a%20) + (a%15) + 18. Therefore i never exceeds its initial bound ((a%20)+10) and qv never exceeds its initial bound ((a%15)+8). Since i is decremented on even k and qv on odd k, the difference i − qv evolves as the initial difference minus (k%2), yielding i − qv = ((a%20)+10) − ((a%15)+8) − (k%2). The loop guard i>0 && qv>0 ensures the loop continues only while these invariants hold and k remains non-negative. int main1(int a){ int k, i, qv; k=0; i=(a%20)+10; qv=(a%15)+8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant k >= 0; loop invariant i <= ((\at(a, Pre) % 20) + 10); loop invariant qv <= ((\at(a, Pre) % 15) + 8); loop invariant i + qv + k == ((\at(a, Pre) % 20) + (\at(a, Pre) % 15) + 18); loop invariant (i + qv) == (a % 20) + (a % 15) + 18 - k; loop invariant i <= (a % 20) + 10; loop invariant qv <= (a % 15) + 8; loop invariant i - qv == ((\at(a,Pre) % 20) + 10) - ((\at(a,Pre) % 15) + 8) - (k % 2); loop assigns i, qv, k; */ for (; i>0&&qv>0; k += 1) { if (k%2==0) { i--; } else { qv--; } } }
rejected: From initialization, k is set to 0, i is initialized to (a%20)+10, and qv is initialized to (a%15)+8, establishing the starting values referenced by the loop. The loop only executes while i>0 and qv>0, so at each loop head I have i>0 and qv>0, hence qv >= 0 and i >= 1 during iterations. In the loop body, k increases by 1 each iteration, and either i decreases by 1 when k is even or qv decreases by 1 when k is odd, so across an iteration i+qv decreases by 1 while k increases by 1, preserving a fixed total pattern. Because i starts at (a%20)+10 and never increases, I conclude i <= ((\at(a, Pre) % 20) + 10) throughout the loop; similarly, since qv starts at (a%15)+8 and never increases, qv <= ((\at(a, Pre) % 15) + 8). The invariant i + qv + k remains equal to ((\at(a, Pre) % 20) + (\at(a, Pre) % 15) + 18) since the initial sum matches and each iteration preserves the sum. Finally, since i, qv, and k are the variables updated inside the loop, this relationship is captured by stating loop assigns i, qv, k. int main1(int a){ int k, i, qv; k=0; i=(a%20)+10; qv=(a%15)+8; /*@ loop invariant qv >= 0; loop invariant i <= ((\at(a, Pre) % 20) + 10); loop invariant qv <= ((\at(a, Pre) % 15) + 8); loop invariant i + qv + k == ((\at(a, Pre) % 20) + (\at(a, Pre) % 15) + 18); loop assigns i, qv, k; */ for (; i>0&&qv>0; k += 1) { if (k%2==0) { i--; } else { qv--; } } }

Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization we have vq = 57, hb = vq, v = -1, c6nc = 0, and xu = hb, which gives -1 <= v <= vq, c6nc >= 0, xu >= 0, hb >= 0, and xu <= 2*vq, with c6nc % 3 == 0; since hb > 0, we also conclude c6nc == 0 and xu == 57 when hb > 0. The loop guard hb > 0 holds initially, so the loop executes and the condition v+1 <= vq is true (since -1 + 1 <= 57), so v is incremented to 0. The line xu = xu + v - v does not change xu because v - v is zero after the update, so xu remains 57 at that point; then c6nc = c6nc + 3 makes c6nc = 3, preserving c6nc % 3 == 0. Next, xu = xu + xu doubles xu from 57 to 114, keeping xu nonnegative and ensuring xu <= 2*vq since 114 <= 114. Finally, hb is set to 0, ending the loop; the implications (hb > 0) ==> (c6nc == 0) and (hb > 0) ==> (xu == 57) are vacuously true after the loop, while the other invariants remain satisfied with v in [-1, vq], c6nc in {0,3}, and xu in {57,114} within bounds. int main1(){ int vq, hb, v, c6nc, xu; vq=57; hb=vq; v=-1; c6nc=0; xu=hb; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant (-1 <= v && v <= vq); loop invariant (0 <= c6nc && 0 <= xu && 0 <= hb && xu <= 2*vq); loop invariant c6nc % 3 == 0; loop invariant (hb > 0) ==> (c6nc == 0); loop invariant (hb > 0) ==> (xu == 57); loop assigns v, xu, c6nc, hb; */ while (hb>0) { if (!(!(v+1<=vq))) { v++; } else { v = vq; } xu = xu+v-v; c6nc = c6nc + 3; xu = xu + xu; hb = 0; } }
rejected: From initialization, v is -1 and vq is 57, so v <= vq holds; c6nc starts at 0, which is divisible by 3; hb starts at 57, so hb >= 0. The loop guard hb > 0 is true initially, so the loop can run. Inside the loop, the condition simplifies to v < vq, so if v < vq we increment v, otherwise we set v to vq; hence v never exceeds vq, preserving v <= vq. Each iteration adds 3 to c6nc, so c6nc remains a multiple of 3, and in particular c6nc % 3 == 0. The updates to xu do not affect these invariants, and finally hb is assigned 0, which keeps hb >= 0; this also provides a decreasing loop variant from the initial 57 down to 0. The loop body assigns v, xu, c6nc, hb, so these variables are precisely those listed in the loop assigns. int main1(){ int vq, hb, v, c6nc, xu; vq=57; hb=vq; // 57 v=-1; c6nc=0; xu=hb; // 57 /*@ loop invariant v <= vq; loop invariant c6nc % 3 == 0; loop invariant hb >= 0; loop variant hb; loop assigns v, xu, c6nc, hb; */ while (hb>0) { if (!(!(v+1<=vq))) { v++; } else { v = vq; } xu = xu+v-v; c6nc = c6nc + 3; xu = xu + xu; hb = 0; } }

Next, xu = xu + xu doubles xu from 57 to 114, keeping xu nonnegative and ensuring xu <= 2*vq since 114 <= 114. Therefore, whenever the loop guard hb > 0 holds, I can reason that xu is 57, i.e., (hb > 0) implies (xu == 57). Inside the loop body, v is updated by either v++ when v+1 <= vq or by v = vq; starting from -1, the condition holds and v becomes 0, and in any case v never exceeds vq, so -1 <= v and v <= vq are maintained. The updates to xu are xu = xu + v - v (which leaves xu unchanged at that moment) and later xu = xu + xu, but hb is set to 0 in the body, so the next loop check will not have hb > 0, preserving the implication (hb > 0) ==> (xu == 57). The loop body explicitly assigns c6nc, hb, v, and xu, so the invariant about those variables being assigned inside the loop is produced. Finally, the loop variant is hb, which starts at 57 and is reduced to 0 by the body, giving a decreasing measure for termination. int main1(){ int vq, hb, v, c6nc, xu; vq=57; hb=vq; v=-1; c6nc=0; xu=hb; /*@ loop invariant (hb > 0) ==> (xu == 57); loop invariant v <= vq; loop invariant -1 <= v; loop assigns c6nc, hb, v, xu; loop variant hb; */ while (hb>0) { if (!(!(v+1<=vq))) { v++; } else { v = vq; } xu = xu+v-v; c6nc = c6nc + 3; xu = xu + xu; hb = 0; } }

chosen: From initialization I derive z >= uq, uq == 0, h == -3, xd >= 0, and z <= 9. I observe that uq is never updated inside the loop, so uq == 0 is preserved as an invariant. The only z update is z = (uq+1) - 1; with uq = 0 this becomes z = 0, so z remains within [0,9] and always satisfies z >= uq. I analyze the if condition; given the initial relation, the else branch executes and assigns xd = z, ensuring xd stays nonnegative. For h, since uq == 0, h += uq does nothing, and the else branch h -= h cannot execute because uq % 6 == 0, so h remains -3. Therefore I conclude the loop invariant set: z >= uq; uq == 0; h == -3; xd >= 0; z <= 9; and the loop assigns xd, h, z. int main1(){ int z, uq, xd, h; z=9; uq=0; xd=z; h=-3; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant z >= uq; loop invariant uq == 0; loop invariant h == -3; loop invariant xd >= 0; loop invariant z <= 9; loop assigns xd, h, z; */ while (uq+1<=z) { if (!(!(xd+1<=z))) { xd++; } else { xd = z; } h += uq; z = (uq+1)-1; if ((uq%6)==0) { h += uq; } else { h -= h; } } }

In the loop body the condition reduces to (xd+1 <= z); if true I increment xd, which preserves xd <= z, and if false I set xd = z, which also yields xd <= z. The update z = uq forces z to become uq in every iteration, so after the body z - uq becomes 0, signaling the variant can only decrease toward termination. The h updates either add uq or set h to zero via h -= h, starting from h = -3, so h remains at least -3 in all cases. Since the body only assigns to xd, h, z, the loop maintains xd <= z and h >= -3 while the variant z - uq remains nonnegative and decreases toward termination. int main1(){ int z, uq, xd, h; z=9; uq=0; xd=z; h=-3; /*@ loop invariant xd <= z; loop invariant h >= -3; loop assigns xd, h, z; loop variant z - uq; */ while (uq+1<=z) { if (!(!(xd+1<=z))) { xd++; } else { xd = z; } h += uq; z = (uq+1)-1; if ((uq%6)==0) { h += uq; } else { h -= h; } } }

Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, dw is set to u+14, mo is 0, aw is 2, and j7 is 6, while u and x remain unchanged from their pre-state. The loop guard uses mo < dw with dw fixed from initialization, and mo starts at 0, so iterations proceed while mo is nonnegative and below dw. In the loop body, mo is incremented by 1, j7 is increased by 4, and aw is increased by 4 on every iteration. Therefore, after each iteration the difference j7 minus aw stays 4 because both grow by 4. Also, since j7 starts at 6 and increases by 4 for each unit increase in mo, j7 equals 6 plus 4 times mo. Finally, dw remains equal to its initial value u plus 14, and u and x stay equal to their pre-state values, giving dw = at(u,Pre) + 14 and u = at(u,Pre) and x = at(x,Pre). int main1(int x,int u){ int dw, mo, aw, j7; dw=u+14; mo=0; aw=2; j7=6; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant j7 - aw == 4; loop invariant dw == \at(u,Pre) + 14; loop invariant j7 == 6 + 4*mo; loop invariant u == \at(u, Pre); loop invariant x == \at(x, Pre); loop invariant 0 <= mo; loop assigns aw, j7, mo; */ while (mo
rejected: I see from initialization that mo is set to 0 and aw is set to 2, so initially aw equals 2 + 4*mo and 0 <= mo holds, while dw is fixed as u+14. The loop guard mo < dw implies at each entry that dw - mo is strictly positive, so dw - mo is nonnegative during iterations. In the loop body mo is incremented by one and aw is increased by four, therefore the quantity aw - 4*mo remains constant and thus aw == 2 + 4*mo is preserved. The body also updates j7 by adding 4 and only aw, j7, and mo are written, so those are the assigned variables of the loop. Since dw is constant and mo increases by one each iteration, dw - mo decreases by exactly one each time, making dw - mo a natural loop variant. int main1(int x,int u){ int dw, mo, aw, j7; dw=u+14; mo=0; aw=2; j7=6; /*@ loop invariant aw == 2 + 4*mo; loop invariant 0 <= mo; loop assigns aw, j7, mo; loop variant dw - mo; */ while (mo < dw) { j7 += 4; mo++; aw += 4; } }

Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: I start from the initialization: na is 16, p2 is 0, and kl is set to na, so at loop entry p2 = 0 and kl = na, which suggests the case p2 == 0 implies kl == na. The loop guard p2 <= na - 1 restricts p2 to the range [0, na-1] at the start of each iteration, and after the body p2 is incremented to p2 + 1, so p2 can reach na after the update. In the loop body, kl is first assigned na - p2 and then increased by 1, yielding kl = na - p2_before + 1, with p2 updated to p2_before + 1. Expressing this in terms of the new p2, I obtain kl = na - p2 + 2, which holds whenever p2 > 0; the edge case after the first update still satisfies this relationship. Combining these observations, I conclude that 0 <= p2 <= na and kl >= 2, with the conditional link that if p2 == 0 then kl == na, and otherwise kl == na - p2 + 2. int main1(){ int na, p2, kl; na=1+15; p2=0; kl=na; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant (0 <= p2 && p2 <= na); loop invariant kl >= 2; loop invariant ((p2 == 0) ==> (kl == na)) && ((p2 != 0) ==> (kl == na - p2 + 2)); loop assigns kl, p2; */ while (p2<=na-1) { kl = na-p2; kl += 1; p2 = p2 + 1; } }
rejected: Initialization sets na to 16, p2 to 0, and kl to na, so at loop entry kl + p2 - na evaluates to 0. The loop guard p2 <= na - 1 implies that at the start of the first iteration p2 is within bounds and hence p2 <= na holds at loop head. Inside the body, kl is recomputed as na - p2 and then incremented, giving kl = na - p2 + 1, and p2 is incremented by 1. Substituting these new values after the body yields kl + p2 - na = (na - p2 + 1) + (p2 + 1) - na = 2. Thus, at the entry to subsequent iterations the value of kl + p2 - na is 2, while initially it was 0, so the invariant that this sum is either 0 or 2 holds throughout. Moreover, because p2 is only increased, p2 <= na holds at loop heads and na - p2 decreases by 1 each iteration, providing a valid loop variant that guarantees termination. int main1(){ int na, p2, kl; na=1+15; p2=0; kl=na; /*@ loop invariant ((kl + p2 - na) == 0) || ((kl + p2 - na) == 2); loop invariant p2 <= na; loop assigns kl, p2; loop variant na - p2; */ while (p2<=na-1) { kl = na-p2; kl += 1; p2 = p2 + 1; } }

The loop guard requires tr < pn2z to execute the body and tr is only increased by one on each iteration, so tr cannot decrease and therefore remains between 0 and pn2z throughout execution. instruction: You are a formal verification expert specializing in loop invariant synthesis for Frama-C with the WP plugin. ## TASK Given a C function with a loop, generate ACSL loop annotations (invariants, assigns) that are inductive and verification-friendly for Frama-C/WP. ## LOOP INVARIANT DEFINITION A loop invariant is a property that: 1. Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From the initial assignments tr is 0 and ky is -6, so I deduce immediately that tr is nonnegative and ky equals -6 when tr == 0. The loop guard requires tr < pn2z to execute the body and tr is only increased by one on each iteration, so tr cannot decrease and therefore remains between 0 and pn2z throughout execution. In the loop body I assign ky = pn2z - tr after incrementing tr, so for any state with tr >= 1 I have ky == pn2z - tr, while the tr == 0 case keeps ky == -6. Because ky is either the initial -6 (which is ≤ pn2z) or set equal to pn2z - tr in the body, I conclude ky ≤ pn2z - tr at all times. Each iteration adds y to z while incrementing tr, so by induction z equals its initial value plus tr times y. int main1(int z,int x){ int pn2z, y, tr, ky; pn2z=141; y=-6; tr=0; ky=-6; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 0 <= tr; loop invariant tr <= pn2z; loop invariant (tr >= 1) ==> (ky == pn2z - tr); loop invariant (tr == 0) ==> (ky == -6); loop invariant z == \at(z,Pre) + tr * y; loop invariant ky <= pn2z - tr; loop assigns tr, z, ky; */ while (1) { if (!(tr
rejected: I see that pn2z is initialized to 141 and tr to 0, so tr <= pn2z holds at loop entry. I also observe ky starts at -6, which confirms the special case (tr == 0) implies (ky == -6). The loop guard makes the loop run only while tr < pn2z, so across iterations tr remains bounded by pn2z and only attains pn2z when exiting. In the loop body I first increment tr by 1, then assign ky to pn2z - tr, so after the assignment ky equals pn2z - tr. Therefore, whenever tr >= 1, I have ky == pn2z - tr, i.e., (tr >= 1) implies (ky == pn2z - tr). The initial state also satisfies (tr == 0) implies (ky == -6), and after the first iteration ky becomes pn2z - tr, so ky <= pn2z - tr holds in all states. int main1(int z,int x){ int pn2z, y, tr, ky; pn2z=141; y=-6; tr=0; ky=-6; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant tr <= pn2z; loop invariant (tr >= 1) ==> (ky == pn2z - tr); loop invariant (tr == 0) ==> (ky == -6); loop invariant ky <= pn2z - tr; loop assigns tr, z, ky; */ while (1) { if (!(tr

chosen: From initialization, I see dh2 is defined as (z % 15) + 10, db is 0, and s3 is initialized to dh2, so the relation s3 = dh2 - db holds initially. Also, since z has not been updated yet, z equals the pre-state of z, at(z,Pre), and with db = 0 this gives z = at(z,Pre) + (db*(db+1)/2). When the body runs, db is incremented by 1 and s3 is reassigned as dh2 - db, hence the invariant s3 = dh2 - db continues to hold after each iteration. Also, z is increased by the new value of db, so starting from z = at(z,Pre) + T(db) before the iteration, we get z = at(z,Pre) + T(db+1) after, maintaining z = at(z,Pre) + (db*(db+1)/2). Since dh2 is not modified inside the loop, the relation dh2 == ((\at(z,Pre) % 15) + 10) holds throughout. Because z grows by db each time and db tracks the number of iterations, the invariant z == (\at(z,Pre)) + (db * (db + 1) / 2) remains preserved. The loop variant dh2 - db decreases by 1 each iteration and stays non-negative until termination when db reaches dh2. int main1(int z,int t){ int dh2, db, s3; dh2=(z%15)+10; db=0; s3=dh2; /*@ loop invariant dh2 == ((\at(z,Pre) % 15) + 10); loop invariant z == (\at(z,Pre)) + (db * (db + 1) / 2); loop variant (dh2 - db); loop assigns db, s3, z; */ while (1) { if (!(db

Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Combined with loop exit (`!guard`), helps establish required loop-exit facts. ## RULES - Add one core conservation/relational equality that explains loop updates (state transition law). - Add minimal progress bounds for loop counters and key arithmetic variables. - Add complete and exact `loop assigns`. - Prefer strong invariants; avoid weak/tautological ones. - Do not add unrelated invariants just to increase count. - Keep original code unchanged except loop annotations. - MUST NOT modify or rewrite any `unknown()`/`unknownN()` call. - `\at(v, Pre)` can ONLY be used with function parameters, never with local variables initialized inside the function. - Loop guard is NOT an invariant; weaken strict guards to non-strict bounds at exit. - In ACSL, `==` binds tighter than `<`/`>`; add parentheses where needed. - Use `loop invariant` only as a line prefix, never inside expressions. ## FORBIDDEN CONSTRUCTS | Forbidden | Reason | |-----------|--------| | `\forall`, `\exists` | Quantifiers not allowed in loop invariants | | `predicate`, `logic`, `axiomatic`, `lemma` | Custom definitions not supported | | Ternary `? :` | Not valid in ACSL annotations | | `\at(local_var, Pre)` | `\at(v, Pre)` works ONLY on function parameters | | `^` for exponentiation | `^` is bitwise XOR in C/ACSL; write `x*x*x` for x cubed | | Boolean in arithmetic | `x + (a > b)` is invalid; use `==>` to split cases | | Undefined variables | Only use variables declared in the given function | ## ALLOWED OPERATORS - Comparison: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!`, `==>`, `<==>` - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` ## ANNOTATION FORMAT ``` /*@ loop invariant ; loop assigns ; loop variant ; // optional */ while (...) { ... } ``` ## OUTPUT First, reason about your invariant choices inside ... tags. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: From initialization, we have s = 0, ia = 0, and j8 = 2, with hp = 5*t, so the starting state assigns s and ia to zero and sets j8. Therefore the relation 6*ia == s*(2*hp - s + 3) holds at the start because both sides evaluate to zero. The loop guard s <= hp - 1 keeps the loop executing while s is less than hp, and since s starts at 0 and increases by 3 each iteration, we maintain 0 <= s throughout. Inside the loop, j8 is assigned hp - s_old, s becomes s_old + 3, and ia becomes ia_old + j8, so ia is updated by hp - s_old. If the prior state satisfies 6*ia_old = s_old*(2*hp - s_old + 3), then after the updates with s' = s_old + 3 and ia' = ia_old + (hp - s_old), a straightforward algebraic substitution shows 6*ia' = s'*(2*hp - s' + 3) also holds. Since s starts at 0 and advances in steps of 3, we also have s % 3 == 0, and the nondecreasing updates preserve 0 <= s. int main1(int g,int t){ int hp, c8, s, j8, ia; hp=t*5; c8=0; s=0; j8=2; ia=c8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 6*ia == s*(2*hp - s + 3); loop invariant s % 3 == 0; loop invariant 0 <= s; loop assigns s, ia, j8; */ while (s<=hp-1) { j8 = hp-s; s = s + 3; ia += j8; } }
rejected: I observe that the initialization sets hp = t*5, s = 0, ia = c8 = 0, and j8 = 2, so at loop entry s = 0 and ia = 0. The loop guard s <= hp - 1 implies s < hp, so s increases but remains below hp during the loop. In each iteration, j8 is assigned hp - s, then s is increased by 3 and ia is increased by j8, i.e., by hp - s_before. After k iterations, s = 3k and ia equals the sum over i from 0 to k-1 of (hp - 3i). Evaluating that sum gives ia = (s/3) * hp - 3 * (((s/3) - 1) * (s/3)) / 2, substituting k = s/3. Since s starts at 0 and increments by 3, s >= 0 holds; and hp - s decreases by 3 each iteration, providing hp - s as a loop variant. int main1(int g,int t){ int hp, c8, s, j8, ia; hp=t*5; c8=0; s=0; j8=2; ia=c8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant ia == ((s/3) * hp) - 3 * (((s/3) - 1) * (s/3)) / 2; loop invariant s >= 0; loop assigns ia, s, j8; loop variant hp - s; */ while (s<=hp-1) { j8 = hp-s; s = s + 3; ia += j8; } }

During each iteration, j8 is computed as hp - s, then s becomes s + 3 and ia becomes ia + j8. Thus the updated values satisfy s' = s + 3 and ia' = ia + (hp - s). If the invariant held before, substituting these into 6*ia' gives 6*ia' = 6*ia + 6hp - 6s, which, using 6*ia = s*(2hp - s + 3), becomes 6*ia' = (s+3)*(2hp - (s+3) + 3). Therefore 6*ia' equals s'*(2hp - s' + 3), proving the invariant 6*ia == s*(2*hp - s + 3) is preserved across iterations. Also since s starts at 0 and is increased by 3 each loop, s remains divisible by 3, giving the invariant s % 3 == 0 throughout. int main1(int g,int t){ int hp, c8, s, j8, ia; hp=t*5; c8=0; s=0; j8=2; ia=c8; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant 6*ia == s*(2*hp - s + 3); loop invariant s % 3 == 0; loop assigns s, ia, j8; */ while (s<=hp-1) { j8 = hp-s; s = s + 3; ia += j8; } }

Holds before the first loop iteration (initialization). 2. Is preserved by every loop iteration when the loop guard is true (inductiveness). 3. Then, return the complete annotated C function inside ... tags.
chosen: On initialization hvd is assigned t+25, so I derive hvd == t + 25 and, since t is not modified anywhere, hvd == \at(t,Pre) + 25. Also l9h is set equal to hvd, hence l9h - hvd == 0 and therefore l9h <= hvd holds at start. With v4 == 0 and t5 == 5 initially and l9h - hvd == 0 I obtain v4 == 5*(l9h - hvd) and t5 == 5 + 5*(l9h - hvd). In the loop body l9h is incremented by 1 while v4 and t5 are incremented by 5, so the difference v4 - 5*(l9h - hvd) remains constant and thus v4 == 5*(l9h - hvd) is preserved. Similarly t5 - 5 - 5*(l9h - hvd) remains constant, preserving t5 == 5 + 5*(l9h - hvd). Finally hvd is never assigned in the loop so hvd == t + 25 stays true, and the loop guard l9h <= hvd - 1 on entry implies after l9h++ we still have l9h <= hvd, preserving l9h <= hvd. int main1(int t){ int hvd, l9h, v4, t5; hvd=t+25; l9h=hvd; v4=0; t5=5; /* >>> LOOP INVARIANT TO FILL <<< */ /*@ loop invariant hvd == t + 25; loop invariant v4 == 5 * (l9h - hvd); loop invariant t5 == 5 + 5 * (l9h - hvd); loop invariant hvd == \at(t,Pre) + 25; loop invariant l9h <= hvd; loop assigns v4, l9h, t5; */ while (l9h<=hvd-1) { v4 = v4 + 5; l9h += 1; t5 = t5 + 5; } }
rejected: I observe that initialization sets hvd to t+25, l9h to hvd, v4 to 0, and t5 to 5, so hvd equals t+25 at loop start and is not changed inside the loop. Since hvd is not updated in the loop, hvd == t+25 remains true throughout execution. At loop entry, l9h equals hvd and v4 equals 0, so l9h − hvd equals 0 and v4 equals 0, aligning with the targeted relation. Each iteration increments v4 by 5 and increments l9h by 1, so after k iterations we have l9h = hvd + k and v4 = 5k. Therefore v4 = 5*(l9h − hvd) holds after any number of iterations, establishing the inferred invariant. int main1(int t){ int hvd, l9h, v4, t5; hvd=t+25; l9h=hvd; v4=0; t5=5; /* @ loop invariant hvd == t + 25; loop invariant v4 == 5 * (l9h - hvd); loop assigns v4, l9h, t5; */ while (l9h<=hvd-1) { v4 = v4 + 5; l9h += 1; t5 = t5 + 5; } }

With v4 == 0 and t5 == 5 initially and l9h - hvd == 0 I obtain v4 == 5*(l9h - hvd) and t5 == 5 + 5*(l9h - hvd). Since hvd remains unchanged and the assignments update v4, l9h, t5 accordingly, the two linear relationships continue to hold as the loop proceeds while hvd − l9h decreases. int main1(int t){ int hvd, l9h, v4, t5; hvd=t+25; l9h=hvd; v4=0; t5=5; /*@ loop invariant v4 == 5 * (l9h - hvd); loop invariant t5 == 5 + 5 * (l9h - hvd); loop assigns v4, l9h, t5; loop variant hvd - l9h; */ while (l9h<=hvd-1) { v4 = v4 + 5; l9h += 1; t5 = t5 + 5; } }
